Formally called as the President of Council of Ministers 2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** As Head of Government, Prime Minister, Secretary of State in 1925. 3. **[^](#cite_ref-3)** While the Kingdom of Italy ended in 1946, De Gasperi continued as Prime Minister of the Republic until 1953. | |

([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Regno d'Italia*, pronounced [[ˈreɲɲo diˈtaːlja]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) was a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") that existed from 17 March 1861, when [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II") of [Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720-1861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720-1861)") was [proclaimed King of Italy](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy"), until 18 June 1946, when the monarchy was abolished, following civil discontent that led to an [institutional referendum on 2 June](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum"), resulting in the establishment of the modern [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic "Italian Republic"). The kingdom was established through the unification of several states over a decades-long process, called the *[Risorgimento](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy")*. That process was influenced by the [Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy")-led [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)"), which was one of Italy's legal [predecessor states](/wiki/Succession_of_states "Succession of states").

The **Kingdom of Italy** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Regno d'Italia*, pronounced [[ˈreɲɲo diˈtaːlja]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")) was a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") that existed from 17 March 1861, when [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II") of [Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720-1861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720-1861)") was [proclaimed King of Italy](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy"), until 18 June 1946, when the monarchy was abolished, following civil discontent that led to an [institutional referendum on 2 June](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum"), resulting in the establishment of the modern [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic "Italian Republic"). The kingdom was established through the unification of several states over a decades-long process, called the *[Risorgimento](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy")*. That process was influenced by the [Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy")-led [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)"), which was one of Italy's legal [predecessor states](/wiki/Succession_of_states "Succession of states").

In 1866, Italy [declared war](/wiki/Third_Italian_War_of_Independence "Third Italian War of Independence") on [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") in [alliance with](/wiki/Italo-Prussian_Alliance "Italo-Prussian Alliance") [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") and, upon its victory, received the region of [Veneto](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto"). Italian troops [entered Rome](/wiki/Capture_of_Rome "Capture of Rome") in 1870, ending [more than one thousand years of Papal temporal power](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"). In the last two decades of the 19th century, Italy developed into a colonial power, resulting to the establishment of the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"), and in 1882 it entered into a [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") with the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire"), following strong disagreements with [France](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic") about their respective colonial expansions. Although relations with [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") became very friendly, the alliance with [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") remained purely formal, due in part to Italy's desire to acquire [Trentino](/wiki/Trentino "Trentino") and [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") from Austria-Hungary. As a result, Italy accepted the [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") invitation to join the [Allied Powers](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), as the western powers promised territorial compensation (at the expense of Austria-Hungary) for participation that was more generous than Vienna's offer in exchange for Italian neutrality. [Victory in the war](/wiki/Big_Four_(World_War_I) "Big Four (World War I)") gave Italy a permanent seat in the Council of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"), but it [did not receive all the territories it was promised](/wiki/Mutilated_victory "Mutilated victory").

In 1922, [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") became prime minister and the [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party") took control of the Italian government, thus ushering an era of the Fascist period in Italy known as "[Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy")". Authoritarian rule was enforced, crushing all political opposition while promoting economic modernization, traditional values, and [territorial expansion](/wiki/Italian_irredentism "Italian irredentism"). In 1929, the Italian government reconciled with the Roman Catholic Church through the [Lateran Treaties](/wiki/Lateran_Treaties "Lateran Treaties"), which granted independence to the [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"). The following decade presided over an [aggressive foreign policy](/wiki/Spazio_vitale "Spazio vitale"), with Italy launching successful military operations against [Ethiopia](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War") in 1935, [Spain](/wiki/Italian_military_intervention_in_Spain "Italian military intervention in Spain") in 1937, and [Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania") in 1939. This led to economic sanctions, departure from the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"), growing economic [autarky](/wiki/Autarky "Autarky"), and the signing of [military](/wiki/Pact_of_Steel "Pact of Steel") [alliances](/wiki/Anti-Comintern_Pact "Anti-Comintern Pact") with [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan").

Italy entered [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") as a leading member of the [Axis Powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") in 1940 and despite initial success, was defeated in [North Africa](/wiki/North_African_campaign "North African campaign") and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Italian_participation_on_the_Eastern_Front "Italian participation on the Eastern Front"). [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") [landings in Sicily](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Sicily "Allied invasion of Sicily") led to the [fall of the Fascist regime](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy") and the new government [surrendered](/wiki/Armistice_of_Cassibile "Armistice of Cassibile") to the Allies in September 1943. German forces occupied northern and central Italy, established the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic"), and reappointed Mussolini as dictator. Consequentially, Italy descended into [civil war](/wiki/Italian_Civil_War "Italian Civil War"), with the [Italian Co-belligerent Army](/wiki/Italian_Co-belligerent_Army "Italian Co-belligerent Army") and [resistance movement](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement") contending with the [Social Republic's forces](/wiki/National_Republican_Army "National Republican Army") and its German allies. Shortly after the [surrender of all Axis forces in Italy](/wiki/Surrender_of_Caserta "Surrender of Caserta"), civil discontent prompted an [institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum"), which established a [republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic "Italian Republic") and abolished the monarchy in 1946.

## Overview

### Territory

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Kingdom_of_Italy_1924_map.svg/250px-Kingdom_of_Italy_1924_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kingdom_of_Italy_1924_map.svg)

The Kingdom of Italy in 1924

The Kingdom of Italy covered and at times exceeded the land area of present-day Italy. The Kingdom gradually extended its area through the [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") until 1870. In 1919 it annexed [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") and [Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige/S%C3%BCdtirol "Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol"). The [Triple Entente](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente") promised to grant to Italy – if the state joined the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") – several regions, including former [Austrian Littoral](/wiki/Austrian_Littoral "Austrian Littoral"), western parts of former [Duchy of Carniola](/wiki/Duchy_of_Carniola "Duchy of Carniola"), Northern [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") and notably [Zara](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar"), [Šibenik](/wiki/%C5%A0ibenik "Šibenik") and most of the Dalmatian islands (except [Krk](/wiki/Krk "Krk") and [Rab](/wiki/Rab_(island) "Rab (island)")), according to the secret [London Pact](/wiki/London_Pact "London Pact") of 1915.[[2]](#cite_note-firstworldwar.com-5)

After the refusal by President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") to acknowledge the London Pact and the signing of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") in 1919, with the [Treaty of Rapallo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rapallo_(1920) "Treaty of Rapallo (1920)") in 1920 Italian claims on Northern Dalmatia were abandoned. During World War II, the Kingdom gained additional territory in Slovenia ([province of Lubiana](/wiki/Province_of_Lubiana "Province of Lubiana")) and Dalmatia ([Governatorate of Dalmatia](/wiki/Governatorate_of_Dalmatia "Governatorate of Dalmatia")) from [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") after its breakup in 1941.[[3]](#cite_note-6)

Italy established and maintained until the end of World War II colonies, protectorates, military occupations and puppet states beyond its borders. These included [Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea"), [Italian Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland"), [Libya](/wiki/Italian_Libya "Italian Libya"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Italian_Ethiopia "Italian Ethiopia") (annexed by Italy from 1936 to 1941), [Albania](/wiki/Albanian_Kingdom_(1939%E2%80%931943) "Albanian Kingdom (1939–1943)") (an Italian protectorate since 1939), [British Somaliland](/wiki/British_Somaliland "British Somaliland"), [part of Greece](/wiki/Axis_occupation_of_Greece "Axis occupation of Greece"), Corsica, southern France with [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), [Tunisia](/wiki/French_Tunisia "French Tunisia"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") and [Montenegro](/wiki/Italian_governorate_of_Montenegro "Italian governorate of Montenegro") (all territories occupied in World War II) [Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia") (Italian and German client state in World War II), and a 46-[hectare](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") concession from [China](/wiki/China "China") in [Tianjin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin") (see [Italian concession in Tianjin](/wiki/Concessions_in_Tianjin#Italian_concession "Concessions in Tianjin")).[[4]](#cite_note-7) These foreign colonies and lands came under Italian control at different times and remained so over different periods.

### Government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Statuto_Albertino_Avviso.jpg/250px-Statuto_Albertino_Avviso.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statuto_Albertino_Avviso.jpg)

Notice of the proclamation of the [Statuto Albertino](/wiki/Statuto_Albertino "Statuto Albertino") in 1848 by King [Charles Albert of Sardinia](/wiki/Charles_Albert_of_Sardinia "Charles Albert of Sardinia")

The Kingdom of Italy was a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"). Executive power belonged to the [monarch](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy"), who governed through [appointed](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") [ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_Italy "Council of Ministers of Italy"). The legislative branch was a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Italy "Parliament of Italy") comprising an appointive [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Senate of the Kingdom of Italy") and an elective [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Italian_Chamber_of_Deputies "Italian Chamber of Deputies"). The kingdom maintained as its [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution") the [Statuto Albertino](/wiki/Statuto_Albertino "Statuto Albertino"), the governing document of the Kingdom of Sardinia. In theory, ministers were responsible solely to the king. However, by this time, a king couldn't appoint a government of his choosing or keep it in office against the express will of Parliament.

Members of the Chamber of Deputies were elected through a [plurality voting system](/wiki/Plurality_voting_system "Plurality voting system") in [single-member](/wiki/Uninominal "Uninominal") districts. A candidate needed the support of 50% of votes and 25% of all enrolled voters to be elected in the first round of ballots. Seats not adjudicated on the first ballot, were filled through a runoff held shortly after the first ballots. In addition to this, there was a [Council of State](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Italy) "Council of State (Italy)"), which had consultative powers and decided on conflicts of jurisdiction between administrative authorities and courts, as well as on disputes between the state and its creditors. It consisted of a president, three section presidents, 24 councilors of state and the service staff, and was appointed by the king on the proposal of the Council of Ministers.

There was brief experimentation in 1882 with multi-member districts, and after World War I [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation") was introduced with large, regional, multi-seat electoral constituencies. The [Socialists](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") became the major party, but were unable to form a government in a parliament split among the three factions of Socialists, Christian [populists](/wiki/Populism "Populism"), and [classical liberals](/wiki/Classical_liberalism "Classical liberalism"). Elections took place in 1919, 1921 and 1924: on this last occasion, Mussolini abolished proportional representation, replacing it with the [Acerbo Law](/wiki/Acerbo_Law "Acerbo Law"), by which the party that won the largest share of votes got two-thirds of the seats, which gave the Fascist Party an absolute majority of the Chamber seats.

Between 1925 and 1943, Italy was a quasi-*[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* [fascist](/wiki/Italian_Fascism "Italian Fascism") dictatorship, as the constitution formally remained in effect without alteration while the monarchy formally accepted Fascist policies and institutions. In 1928 the [Grand Council of Fascism](/wiki/Grand_Council_of_Fascism "Grand Council of Fascism") took control of government administration, and in 1939 the [Chamber of Fasces and Corporations](/wiki/Chamber_of_Fasces_and_Corporations "Chamber of Fasces and Corporations") replaced the Chamber of Deputies.

The highest state administration was divided into the following ministries, with their headquarters in Rome:

* [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Italy) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Italy)") (with the Council for Diplomatic Disputes)
* [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Italy) "Ministry of the Interior (Italy)") (with the Supreme Health Council)
* [Ministry of Mercy, Justice and Religious Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(Italy) "Ministry of Justice (Italy)")
* [Ministry of Finance and Treasury](/wiki/Ministry_of_Economy_and_Finance_(Italy) "Ministry of Economy and Finance (Italy)")
* [Ministry of War](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Italy) "Ministry of Defense (Italy)") (the committees for the General Staff, the Line Arms, the Royal Carabiniers, for Artillery and Engineering, for Military Health and the Supreme War and Naval Tribunal are subordinate to it)
* [Ministry of the Navy](/wiki/Ministry_of_Defense_(Italy)#History "Ministry of Defense (Italy)") (with the Supreme Navy Council)
* [Ministry of Public Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education,_Universities_and_Research_(Italy) "Ministry of Education, Universities and Research (Italy)") (with the Higher Education Council)
* [Ministry of Public Works](/wiki/Ministry_of_Public_Works_(Italy) "Ministry of Public Works (Italy)") (with the Higher Council for Public Works and the Railway Council)
* [Ministry of Agriculture, Industry and Trade](/w/index.php?title=Ministry_of_Agricultural,_Food_and_Forestry_Policy_(Italy)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ministry of Agricultural, Food and Forestry Policy (Italy) (page does not exist)") (with the Mining Council and the Central Statistical Giunta)
* [Ministry of Colonies](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Colonies_(Italy) "Ministry of the Colonies (Italy)") (since 1937 Ministry for Italian Africa)
* Ministry for the Liberated Territories (for the territories occupied and annexed after the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"))
* [Ministry of Transport](/wiki/Ministry_of_Infrastructure_and_Transport_(Italy) "Ministry of Infrastructure and Transport (Italy)")
* Ministry of Aviation
* [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education,_Universities_and_Research_(Italy) "Ministry of Education, Universities and Research (Italy)")
* Ministry of Post and Telegraph
* [Ministry of Labour](/wiki/Ministry_of_Labour_and_Social_Policies_(Italy) "Ministry of Labour and Social Policies (Italy)")
* [Ministry of Agriculture](/wiki/Ministry_of_Agriculture,_Food_Sovereignty_and_Forests_(Italy) "Ministry of Agriculture, Food Sovereignty and Forests (Italy)")

Due to the war, a number of other short-lived ministries were created during the [First](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") and [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War").

The [Court of Auditors of the Kingdom](/wiki/Court_of_Audit_(Italy) "Court of Audit (Italy)") had an independent status.

### Military structure

* [King of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy") – [supreme commander](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the Italian Royal Army, Navy and later Air Force from 1861 to 1938 and 1943 to 1946
* [First Marshal of the Empire](/wiki/First_Marshal_of_the_Empire "First Marshal of the Empire") – [supreme commander](/wiki/Generalissimo "Generalissimo") of the Italian Royal Army, Air Force, Navy and the Voluntary Militia for National Security from 1938 to 1943 during the Fascist era, held by both [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III "Victor Emmanuel III") and [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini")
* *[Regio Esercito](/wiki/Regio_Esercito "Regio Esercito")* (Royal Italian Army)
* *[Regia Marina](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina")* (Royal Italian Navy)
* *[Regia Aeronautica](/wiki/Regia_Aeronautica "Regia Aeronautica")* (Royal Italian Air Force)
* *[Milizia Volontaria per la Sicurezza Nazionale](/wiki/Milizia_Volontaria_per_la_Sicurezza_Nazionale "Milizia Volontaria per la Sicurezza Nazionale")* (Voluntary Militia for National Security also known as the "MVSN" or "[Blackshirts](/wiki/Blackshirts "Blackshirts")") – militia loyal to Mussolini during the Fascist era, abolished in 1943.

### Monarchs

Main article: [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Iron_Crown.JPG/250px-Iron_Crown.JPG)](/wiki/File:Iron_Crown.JPG)

The [Iron Crown of Lombardy](/wiki/Iron_Crown_of_Lombardy "Iron Crown of Lombardy"), for centuries a symbol of the [kings of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy")

The [King of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy") was formally the holder of state power, but he could only exercise the right of [legislation](/wiki/Legislation "Legislation") in conjunction with the national parliament, and the government was de facto responsible to parliament. According to the [Salic Law](/wiki/Lex_Salica "Lex Salica"), the throne was inherited in the male line of the royal [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"). The king and his house professed allegiance to the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"). He came of age at the age of 18 and, upon assuming power, took an [oath to the constitution](/wiki/Oath_of_office "Oath of office") in the presence of both chambers. According to the law of 17 March 1861, his title was: "[By the grace of God](/wiki/Divine_right_of_kings "Divine right of kings") and by the will of the nation, King of Italy and King of Albania (only from 1939 to 1943) and Emperor of Ethiopia (only from 1936 to 1943)". He awarded the five [Orders of Knighthood](/wiki/Orders_of_Knighthood "Orders of Knighthood") of Savoy and exercised constitutional sovereign rights. He commanded the land, sea and air power; he declared wars, concluded peace, alliance, trade and other treaties, of which only those that entailed a burden on finances or a change in territory required the approval of the chambers to be effective. The king appointed to all state offices, [sanctioned](/wiki/Pragmatic_sanction "Pragmatic sanction") and promulgated the laws, which as well as the government acts had to be [countersigned](/wiki/Countersign_(legal) "Countersign (legal)") by the responsible ministers, and issued the [decrees](/wiki/Decrees "Decrees") and [regulations](/wiki/Guideline "Guideline") necessary for the execution of the laws. Justice was administered in his name, and he alone had the [pardon](/wiki/Pardon "Pardon") and mitigation of punishment.

### State symbols

The first [state coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Italy "Coat of arms of Italy") of the kingdom was adopted from Sardinia-Piedmont. It included the coat of arms of the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") in the middle and four Italian flags dating from 1848.

On 4 May 1870, by royal decree, two lions in [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), which now carried the [shield](/wiki/Shield "Shield"), a crowned knight's helmet, which bore the [Military Order of Savoy](/wiki/Military_Order_of_Savoy "Military Order of Savoy"), the [Order of the Crown of Italy](/wiki/Order_of_the_Crown_of_Italy "Order of the Crown of Italy"), the [Order of Saints Maurice and Lazarus](/wiki/Order_of_Saints_Maurice_and_Lazarus "Order of Saints Maurice and Lazarus") and the [Order of the Annunciation](/wiki/Order_of_the_Annunciation "Order of the Annunciation") around its collar, were added. The motto [FERT](/wiki/FERT "FERT") was deleted. The lions carried [lances](/wiki/Lances "Lances") that held the national flag. From the helmet fell a royal cloak, which was supposed to protect the nation. Above the coat of arms was the star of Italy (Italian [Stella d'Italia](/wiki/Stella_d%27Italia "Stella d'Italia")).

The newly adopted national coat of arms of 1 January 1890 removed the fur coat and the lances and the crown on the helmet was replaced by the [Iron Crown](/wiki/Iron_Crown "Iron Crown") of the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards"). The whole group stood under a [canopy](/wiki/Mantle_and_pavilion_(heraldry) "Mantle and pavilion (heraldry)"), crowned with the Italian royal crown, above which was the banner of Italy. The flagpole was carried by a golden crowned [eagle](/wiki/Eagle_(heraldry) "Eagle (heraldry)").

On 11 April 1929, Mussolini replaced the two Savoy lions with [lictor's bundles](/wiki/Fascis "Fascis"). Only after his dismissal in 1944 was the old coat of arms from 1890 restored.

## History

Main article: [History of the Kingdom of Italy (1861–1946)](/wiki/History_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy_(1861%E2%80%931946) "History of the Kingdom of Italy (1861–1946)")

### Unification process (1848–1870)

Main article: [Italian Unification](/wiki/Italian_Unification "Italian Unification")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Italian-unification.gif/250px-Italian-unification.gif)](/wiki/File:Italian-unification.gif)

Animated map of the [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") from 1829 to 1871

The [birth of the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Birth_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Birth of the Kingdom of Italy") was the result of efforts by Italian nationalists and monarchists loyal to the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") to establish a united kingdom encompassing the entire [Italian Peninsula](/wiki/Italian_Peninsula "Italian Peninsula"). Following the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1815, the political and social [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") movement, or *Risorgimento*, emerged to unite Italy consolidating the different states of the peninsula and liberate it from foreign control. A prominent radical figure was the patriotic journalist [Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini"), member of the secret revolutionary society of *[Carbonari](/wiki/Carbonari "Carbonari")* and founder of the influential political movement [Young Italy](/wiki/Young_Italy_(historical) "Young Italy (historical)") in the early 1830s. Mazzini favoured a unitary republic and advocated a broad nationalist movement. His prolific output of propaganda helped to spread the unification movement.

The most famous member of Young Italy was the revolutionary and general [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi"), renowned for his extremely loyal followers,[[5]](#cite_note-8) who led the Italian republican drive for unification in Southern Italy. However, the Northern Italy monarchy of the House of Savoy in the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)"), whose government was led by [Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour"), also had ambitions of establishing a united Italian state. In the context of the [1848 liberal revolutions](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848") that swept through Europe, an unsuccessful [First Italian War of Independence](/wiki/First_Italian_War_of_Independence "First Italian War of Independence"), led by King [Charles Albert of Sardinia](/wiki/Charles_Albert_of_Sardinia "Charles Albert of Sardinia"), was declared on [Austria](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"). In 1855, the Kingdom of Sardinia became an ally of Britain and France in the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War"), giving Cavour's diplomacy legitimacy in the eyes of the great powers.[[6]](#cite_note-9)[[7]](#cite_note-10) The Kingdom of Sardinia again attacked the Austrian Empire in the [Second Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Second_Italian_War_of_Independence "Second Italian War of Independence") of 1859, with the aid of [France](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire"), resulting in liberating [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy"). On the basis of the secret [Plombières Agreement](/wiki/Plombi%C3%A8res_Agreement "Plombières Agreement") (21 July 1858), the Kingdom of Sardinia ceded [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") and [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") to France, an event that caused the [Niçard exodus](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard_exodus "Niçard exodus"), that was the emigration of a quarter of the [Niçard Italians](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard_Italians "Niçard Italians") to Italy.[[8]](#cite_note-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Giuseppe_Mazzini.jpg/250px-Giuseppe_Mazzini.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Mazzini.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Giuseppe_Garibaldi_1861.jpg/250px-Giuseppe_Garibaldi_1861.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giuseppe_Garibaldi_1861.jpg)

[Giuseppe Mazzini](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini") (left), highly influential leader of the Italian revolutionary movement; and [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") (right), celebrated as one of the greatest generals of modern times[[9]](#cite_note-scholar_and_patriot-12) and as the "Hero of the Two Worlds" because of his military enterprises in South America and Europe,[[10]](#cite_note-Garibaldi_on_Encyclopædia_Britannica-13) who fought in many military campaigns that led to [Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification")

In 1860–1861, Garibaldi led the drive for unification in Naples and Sicily (the [Expedition of the Thousand](/wiki/Expedition_of_the_Thousand "Expedition of the Thousand")),[[11]](#cite_note-14) while the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") troops occupied the central territories of the Italian peninsula, except Rome and part of [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"). [Teano](/wiki/Teano "Teano") was the site of the famous meeting of 26 October 1860 between Giuseppe Garibaldi and [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel II of Italy"), last King of Sardinia, in which Garibaldi shook Victor Emanuel's hand and hailed him as [King of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy"); thus, Garibaldi sacrificed republican hopes for the sake of Italian unity under a monarchy. Cavour agreed to include Garibaldi's Southern Italy allowing it to join the union with the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia "Kingdom of Sardinia") in 1860. This allowed the Sardinian government to [declare a united Italian kingdom](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy") on 17 March 1861.[[12]](#cite_note-15) [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II"), since March 1849 King of Sardinia, then became the first king of a united Italy, and the capital was moved from Turin to Florence. The title of "King of Italy" had been out of use since the abdication of [Napoleon I of France](/wiki/Napoleon_I_of_France "Napoleon I of France") on 6 April 1814.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/VictorEmmanuel2.jpg/250px-VictorEmmanuel2.jpg)](/wiki/File:VictorEmmanuel2.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d9/Tuminello%2C_Lodovico_%281824-1907%29_-_Cavour_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tuminello,_Lodovico_(1824-1907)_-_Cavour_cropped.jpg)

[Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel II of Italy") (left) and [Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour") (right), leading figures in the Italian unification, became respectively the first king and first Prime Minister of unified Italy.

Following the unification of most of Italy, tensions between the royalists and republicans erupted. In April 1861, Garibaldi entered the Italian parliament and challenged Cavour's leadership, accusing him of dividing Italy, and threatened a civil war between the Kingdom in the North and his forces in the South. On 6 June 1861, the Kingdom's strongman Cavour died. During the ensuing political instability, Garibaldi and the republicans became increasingly revolutionary in tone. Garibaldi's arrest in 1862 set off worldwide controversy.[[13]](#cite_note-16)

In 1866, [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck"), [Minister President of Prussia](/wiki/Minister_President_of_Prussia "Minister President of Prussia"), offered Victor Emmanuel II [an alliance](/wiki/Italo-Prussian_Alliance "Italo-Prussian Alliance") with the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") in the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War"). In exchange, Prussia would allow Italy to annex Austria-controlled [Veneto](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto"). King Emmanuel agreed to the alliance, and the [Third Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Third_Italian_War_of_Independence "Third Italian War of Independence") began. Italy fared poorly in the war with a badly-organized military against Austria, but Prussia's victory allowed Italy to [annex Veneto](/wiki/Plebiscite_of_Veneto_of_1866 "Plebiscite of Veneto of 1866"). At this point, one major obstacle to Italian unity remained: Rome.

In July 1870, Prussia went to war with France, igniting the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War"). To keep the large [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") at bay, France abandoned its positions in Rome – which protected the remnants of the Papal States and [Pius IX](/wiki/Pius_IX "Pius IX") – to fight the Prussians. Italy benefited from Prussia's victory against France by taking over the Papal States from French authority. The Kingdom of Italy [captured Rome](/wiki/Capture_of_Rome "Capture of Rome") after several battles and guerrilla-like warfare by [Papal Zouaves](/wiki/Papal_Zouaves "Papal Zouaves") and official troops of the Holy See against the Italian invaders. Italy's unification was completed and its capital moved to Rome. Victor Emmanuel, Garibaldi, Cavour, and Mazzini are remembered as Italy's *Four Fathers of the Fatherland*.[[9]](#cite_note-scholar_and_patriot-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Piazza_Venezia_-_Il_Vittoriano_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Piazza_Venezia_-_Il_Vittoriano_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piazza_Venezia_-_Il_Vittoriano_(cropped).jpg)

The [Altare della Patria](/wiki/Altare_della_Patria "Altare della Patria") in Rome, a [national symbol of Italy](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Italy "National symbols of Italy") celebrating King Victor Emmanuel II, and resting place of the [Italian Unknown Soldier](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Unknown_Soldier_(Italy) "Tomb of the Unknown Soldier (Italy)") since the end of World War I. It was inaugurated in 1911, on the occasion of the 50th [Anniversary of the Unification of Italy](/wiki/Anniversary_of_the_Unification_of_Italy "Anniversary of the Unification of Italy").

Garibaldi was elected in 1871 in his home city of Nice to the French [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(France) "National Assembly (France)"), where he tried to promote the cession of the city from France to the newborn Italian unitary state. He was prevented from speaking,[[14]](#cite_note-17) which led the Garibaldini to riots called the "[Niçard Vespers](/wiki/Ni%C3%A7ard_Vespers "Niçard Vespers")".[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-19) Fifteen of the Nice rebels were tried and sentenced.[[17]](#cite_note-20)

Economic conditions in united Italy were poor.[[18]](#cite_note-21) The country lacked transportation facilities (see [here](/wiki/History_of_rail_transport_in_Italy "History of rail transport in Italy")) and industry, and suffered from extreme poverty (especially in the *[Mezzogiorno](/wiki/Mezzogiorno "Mezzogiorno")*) and high illiteracy. Only a small wealthy elite had the right to vote. The unification movement relied largely on foreign powers' support and continued to do so afterwards. Following the capture of Rome in 1870 from French forces of [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III_of_France "Napoleon III of France"), Papal troops, and [Zouaves](/wiki/Zouave#Papal_Zouaves "Zouave"), relations between Italy and the [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") remained sour for the next sixty years, with the [Popes](/wiki/Pope "Pope") declaring themselves to be [prisoners in the Vatican](/wiki/Prisoner_in_the_Vatican "Prisoner in the Vatican"). The [Catholic Church in Italy](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Italy "Catholic Church in Italy") frequently protested the anti-clerical actions of the secular Italian governments, refused to meet with envoys from the King, and urged Roman Catholics not to vote in Italian elections.[[19]](#cite_note-22) Not until 1929 was the [Roman question](/wiki/Roman_question "Roman question") resolved and positive relations restored between the Kingdom of Italy and the Vatican, after the signing of the [Lateran Pacts](/wiki/Lateran_Treaty "Lateran Treaty").

Some of the states that had been targeted for unification (*[terre irredente](/wiki/Italian_irredentism "Italian irredentism")*), [Trentino-Alto Adige](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige "Trentino-Alto Adige") and the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March"), did not join the Kingdom of Italy until 1918 after Italy [had defeated](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto") [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") at the end of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). For this reason, historians sometimes describe the unification period as continuing past 1871, including activities during the late 19th century and the [First World War](/wiki/Italian_Front_(World_War_I) "Italian Front (World War I)") (1915–1918), and reaching completion only with the [Armistice of Villa Giusti](/wiki/Armistice_of_Villa_Giusti "Armistice of Villa Giusti") on 4 November 1918. This more expansive view of the unification is presented at the [Central Museum of the Risorgimento](/wiki/Central_Museum_of_the_Risorgimento "Central Museum of the Risorgimento").[[20]](#cite_note-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24)

#### Unifying multiple bureaucracies

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Kingdom_of_Italy_1943.png/250px-Kingdom_of_Italy_1943.png)](/wiki/File:Kingdom_of_Italy_1943.png)

Map of the Kingdom of Italy at its greatest extent in 1943, during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), with the [annexation of small territories from France](/wiki/Italian_occupation_of_France "Italian occupation of France") and [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia"). The territories annexed by the latter are the area constituting the [province of Ljubljana](/wiki/Province_of_Ljubljana "Province of Ljubljana"), the area merged with the [province of Fiume](/wiki/Province_of_Fiume "Province of Fiume") and the areas making up the [Governorate of Dalmatia](/wiki/Governorate_of_Dalmatia "Governorate of Dalmatia").

A major challenge for the prime ministers of the new Kingdom of Italy was integrating the political and administrative systems of the seven different major components under a unified set of policies. The different regions were proud of their traditions and could not easily be fitted into the Sardinian model. Cavour started planning for integration, but died (on 6 June 1861) before it was fully developed – indeed, the challenge [is thought to have hastened his early death](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour#Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour"). The regional administrative bureaucracies followed the Napoleonic precedent, so their harmonization was relatively straightforward. The next challenge was to develop a parliamentary legislative system. Cavour and most liberals up and down the peninsula highly admired the [British system](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom"), which became the model for Italy.

Harmonizing the Navy ([Regia Marina](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina")) and the [Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army") was much more complex, chiefly because the systems of [recruiting soldiers](/wiki/Military_recruitment "Military recruitment") and selecting and promoting officers were so different and [grandfathered](/wiki/Grandfather_clause "Grandfather clause") exceptions to the general system persisted for decades. The disorganization helps explain the dismal performance of the Italian navy in the [1866 war](/wiki/Third_Italian_War_of_Independence "Third Italian War of Independence").

Uniformizing the diverse education systems also proved complicated. Shortly before his death, Cavour appointed [Francesco De Sanctis](/wiki/Francesco_De_Sanctis "Francesco De Sanctis") as minister of education, an eminent scholar from the [University of Naples](/wiki/University_of_Naples "University of Naples") who proved an able and patient administrator. The [addition of Veneto in 1866](/wiki/Plebiscite_of_Veneto_of_1866 "Plebiscite of Veneto of 1866") and Rome in 1870 further complicated the challenges of bureaucratic coordination.[[22]](#cite_note-25)

### Economy

See also: [History of coins in Italy](/wiki/History_of_coins_in_Italy "History of coins in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/20_lire_Vittorio_Emanuele_II.jpg/250px-20_lire_Vittorio_Emanuele_II.jpg)](/wiki/File:20_lire_Vittorio_Emanuele_II.jpg)

Golden 20 [lire](/wiki/Italian_lira "Italian lira") coin with the effigy of King [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II") of 1873. Minted in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") (M BN); other mints included [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") (R) and [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") (T BN).

[Italy has a long history of different coinage types](/wiki/History_of_coins_in_Italy "History of coins in Italy"). Italian unification highlighted the confusion of the pre-unification Italian monetary system which was mostly based on silver monometallism and therefore in contrast with the gold monometallism in force in the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") and in the major European nations.[[23]](#cite_note-26) To reconcile the various monetary systems it was decided to opt for [bimetallism](/wiki/Bimetallism "Bimetallism"), taking inspiration from the [French franc](/wiki/French_franc "French franc") model, from which the dimensions of the coins and the exchange rate of 1 to 15.50 between gold and silver were taken. The Italian monetary system, however, differed from the French one in two aspects: silver coins could be exchanged in unlimited quantities with the State, but limited quantities between private individuals and it was decided to mint coins that nominally had 900‰ fine silver, but which in fact they contained 835‰ so as to approach the real exchange rate between gold and silver which was approximately 1 to 14.38.[[24]](#cite_note-27) Exactly four months after the [proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy"), the government introduced the new national currency, the [Italian lira](/wiki/Italian_lira "Italian lira"). The legal tender of the new currency was established by the Royal Decree of 17 July 1861 which specified the exchange of pre-unification coins into lire and the fact that local coins continued to be legal tender in their respective provinces of origin.[[25]](#cite_note-28)

In the entire period from 1861 to 1940, Italy experienced considerable economic growth despite several [economic crises](/wiki/Economic_crises "Economic crises") and the First World War. Unlike most modern nations, where industrialization was undertaken by large [corporations](/wiki/Corporations "Corporations"), industrial growth in Italy was mostly due to small and medium-sized family businesses.

Rail network in Italy 1861–1870

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Italia_ferrovie_1861.03.17.png/120px-Italia_ferrovie_1861.03.17.png)](/wiki/File:Italia_ferrovie_1861.03.17.png)

Network as of 17 March 1861

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Italia_ferrovie_1870_09_20.png/120px-Italia_ferrovie_1870_09_20.png)](/wiki/File:Italia_ferrovie_1870_09_20.png)

Network as of 20 September 1870

Political unification did not automatically bring about [economic integration](/wiki/Economic_integration "Economic integration"), because of the sharp contrasts in culture, politics, and economic practices among the various regions. Italy managed to industrialize in several steps, although the country remained the most backward economy among the great powers (except for the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire")) and was very dependent on [foreign trade](/wiki/Foreign_trade "Foreign trade"), especially the international markets through which it imported [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") and exported [grain](/wiki/Grain "Grain").

After unification, Italy had a predominantly [agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural "Agricultural") society, with 60 percent of the labor force employed in agriculture. Advances in technology increased export opportunities for Italian agricultural produce after a period of crisis in the 1880s. With industrialization, the proportion employed in agriculture fell below 50% around the transition of the 19th into the 20th century. However, not everyone benefited from these developments, as southern agriculture in particular suffered from the hot [arid climate](/wiki/Arid_climate "Arid climate"), while in the north [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria") hampered cultivation of low-lying areas on the [Adriatic](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") coast.

The government's focus on foreign and military policy in the early years of the state led to the neglect of agriculture, which declined after 1873. The Italian parliament initiated an investigation in 1877, which lasted eight years and blamed the lack of [mechanization](/wiki/Mechanization "Mechanization") and modern farming techniques, and the failure of landowners to develop their lands. In addition, most farm laborers were temporary inexperienced short-term workers (*braccianti*). Farmers without a steady income were forced to subsist on meager food. Disease spread rapidly and a major cholera epidemic killed at least 55,000 people. Government action was blocked by strong political and economic opposition from the large landowners. Another commission of inquiry in 1910 found similar problems.

Around 1890 there was also an [overproduction](/wiki/Overproduction "Overproduction") crisis in the [Italian wine industry](/wiki/Italian_wine "Italian wine"), almost the only successful sector in agriculture. In the 1870s and 1880s, viticulture in France suffered from a crop failure caused by insects, and Italy became the largest wine exporter in Europe. After France's recovery in 1888, Italian wine exports collapsed, causing a wave of unemployment and bankruptcies.

From the 1860s, Italy invested heavily in the development of [railways](/wiki/History_of_rail_transport_in_Italy "History of rail transport in Italy"), with its rail network more than tripling between 1861 and 1872, then doubling again by 1890. [Gio. Ansaldo & C.](/wiki/Gio._Ansaldo_%26_C. "Gio. Ansaldo & C.") from the former Kingdom of Sardinia provided the first Italian built locomotives with the [FS Class 113](/wiki/FS_Class_113 "FS Class 113") and the later [FS Class 650](/wiki/FS_Class_650 "FS Class 650"). The first railway section on the island of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") was inaugurated on 28 April 1863 with the [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo")–[Bagheria](/wiki/Bagheria "Bagheria") line. By 1914 the Italian railway had around 17,000 km of railways.

During the Fascist dictatorship, enormous sums were invested in new technological achievements, especially in military technology. But large sums of money were also spent on [prestige](/wiki/Reputation "Reputation") projects such as the construction of the new Italian ocean liner [SS *Rex*](/wiki/SS_Rex "SS Rex"), which set a [transatlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") sea voyage record of four days in 1933, and the development of the seaplane *[Macchi-Castoldi M.C.72](/wiki/Macchi_M.C.72 "Macchi M.C.72")*, which was the world's fastest seaplane in 1933. In 1933, [Italo Balbo](/wiki/Italo_Balbo "Italo Balbo") completed a flight across the Atlantic in a seaplane to the [World's Fair](/wiki/A_Century_of_Progress "A Century of Progress") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"). The flight symbolized the power of the Fascist leadership and the industrial and technological progress the state had made under the Fascists.

### Industrialization

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Italy_Industry_1871.svg/250px-Italy_Industry_1871.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_Industry_1871.svg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/1898-illustraz-italiana-officina-Zopfi-pag-152-foto2.jpg/250px-1898-illustraz-italiana-officina-Zopfi-pag-152-foto2.jpg)](/wiki/File:1898-illustraz-italiana-officina-Zopfi-pag-152-foto2.jpg)

A machinery exposition in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), set in 1898, during the period of industrialization, National Exhibition of Turin, 1898

During the 1860s and 1870s, Italian manufacturing was backward and small-scale, while the oversized agrarian sector was the backbone of the national economy. The country lacked large coal and iron deposits.[[28]](#cite_note-31) In the 1880s, a severe [farm crisis](/wiki/Italian_farm_crisis "Italian farm crisis") led to the introduction of more modern farming techniques in the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley"),[[29]](#cite_note-32) while from 1878 to 1887 [protectionist](/wiki/Protectionist "Protectionist") policies were introduced with the aim of establishing a base of heavy industry.[[30]](#cite_note-33)

In the 1880s industrialisation moved into high gear, which lasted until 1912/13 and reached its peak under [Giolitti](/wiki/Giovanni_Giolitti "Giovanni Giolitti"). Industrial plants soon clustered around areas of [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower") energy.[[31]](#cite_note-34) Between 1887 and 1911 [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") became the main source of energy, with over sixty [plants](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") constructed.[[32]](#cite_note-35) From 1881 to 1887, Italy's textile, mechanical, steel, iron, and chemical industries grew by an average 4.6 percent annually.[[33]](#cite_note-36) The backbone of the industrial boom was, next to the labor force, institutions of higher learning such as the [Politecnico](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Milan "Polytechnic University of Milan") founded in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") in 1863 by [Francesco Brioschi](/wiki/Francesco_Brioschi "Francesco Brioschi") and the [Technical School for Engineers](/wiki/Polytechnic_University_of_Turin "Polytechnic University of Turin") in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") established four years earlier.

Steelworks were established with state and private capital, notably from the [Credito Mobiliare](/wiki/Credito_Mobiliare "Credito Mobiliare"): in 1884 in [Terni](/wiki/Terni "Terni") and in 1897 in [Piombino](/wiki/Piombino "Piombino") using iron-ore from [Elba](/wiki/Elba "Elba"). The relative backwardness of the south continued to be a central problem for the state. Various solutions were proposed for the so-called "[Southern question](/wiki/Southern_question "Southern question")" by [Francesco Saverio Nitti](/wiki/Francesco_Saverio_Nitti "Francesco Saverio Nitti"), [Gaetano Salvemini](/wiki/Gaetano_Salvemini "Gaetano Salvemini") and Sidney Sonnino, but the government only acted in special problem areas such as [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples").[[34]](#cite_note-37) The [ILVA](/wiki/Ilva_(company) "Ilva (company)") group of [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), with the political and financial backing of the Italian state, built the [Bagnoli](/wiki/Bagnoli "Bagnoli") steel plant as part of the 1904 law for the development of [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), prepared by economist and later prime minister Nitti. In 1898, in order to make the steel-industry completely independent from foreign [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal") imports, the [Neapolitan](/wiki/Naples "Naples") engineer Ernesto Stassano invented the [Stassano furnace](/wiki/Stassano_furnace "Stassano furnace"), the first indirect-arc [electric furnace](/wiki/Electric_arc_furnace "Electric arc furnace"). By 1917, Italian iron and steel plants operated 88 indirect-arc furnaces, manufactured by Stassano, Bassanese and Angelini.[[35]](#cite_note-38)

In 1899 the automobile industry commenced when [Giovanni Agnelli](/wiki/Giovanni_Agnelli "Giovanni Agnelli") bought the designs and patents of the [Ceirano brothers](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Ceirano "Giovanni Battista Ceirano") and founded the [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat_S.p.A. "Fiat S.p.A.") automobile works. In 1906 another automobile factory was built in the [Portello](/wiki/Portello_(district_of_Milan) "Portello (district of Milan)") district of Milan for the French entrepreneur [Alexandre Darracq](/wiki/Alexandre_Darracq "Alexandre Darracq") and the headquarters of his Italian branch *Società Anonima Italiana Darracq*. In 1910, the company brought the first successful model onto the market with the [24 HP](/wiki/ALFA_24_HP "ALFA 24 HP") and the brand name *ALFA* on the radiator grille. While automobiles were only affordable by few its popularity and fascination rose rapidly, and one of the first [sports car racing](/wiki/Sports_car_racing "Sports car racing") events in the world, the [Targa Florio](/wiki/Targa_Florio "Targa Florio"), held annually in the mountains of [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") was established in 1906.

In the financial sector, Prime Minister Giolitti was mainly concerned with increasing [pensions](/wiki/Pensions "Pensions") and restructuring the [state budget](/wiki/State_budget "State budget"), though proceeding with great caution. The government secured the support of large companies and banks. Most criticism the project came from conservatives, with a majority of the public supporting the soundness of public finances. The state budget, which from 1900 had an annual income of around 50 million lire, was to be additionally strengthened by the [nationalization](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") of the railways.

In March 1905, after serious labor unrest among railroad workers, Giolitti resigned due to illness, and suggested his fellow party member [Alessandro Fortis](/wiki/Alessandro_Fortis "Alessandro Fortis") to the king as his successor. On 28 March Victor Emmanuel III appointed Fortis as the new prime minister, making him the first [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") head of government worldwide. With Law 137 of 22 April 1905 he sanctioned the nationalization of the railways through a public recruitment process under the control of the [Court of Audit](/wiki/Court_of_Audit_(Italy) "Court of Audit (Italy)") and the supervision of the Ministries of Public Works and Finance. At the same time, the [telephone](/wiki/Telephone "Telephone") system was nationalized.[[36]](#cite_note-Gernert_et_al._2016,_p._369-39) The Fortis government remained in office until the beginning of 1906. It was followed from 8 February to 29 May by a brief government under [Sidney Sonnino](/wiki/Sidney_Sonnino "Sidney Sonnino"). Finally, Giolitti entered his third term. In this he dealt mainly with the economic situation in southern Italy, due both to long-term demographic and economic factors, as well as natural disasters such as the eruption of [Vesuvius](/wiki/Vesuvius "Vesuvius") in 1906 and the [earthquake](/wiki/1908_Messina_earthquake "1908 Messina earthquake") in [Messina](/wiki/Messina "Messina"), [Calabria](/wiki/Reggio_Calabria "Reggio Calabria"), and [Palmi](/wiki/Palmi,_Calabria "Palmi, Calabria") in 1908. Entire villages were depopulated and centuries-old regional cultures disappeared.[[36]](#cite_note-Gernert_et_al._2016,_p._369-39) Nevertheless, there was a slight economic upswing in the south afterwards. The government, which had initially discouraged emigration to avoid [labor shortages](/wiki/Labor_market "Labor market"), now gave its approval to the emigration of hundreds of thousands of Italians from the south. This was motivated by fear of increasing social tensions and monetary instability.

In 1906 the government lowered the national [interest tax](/wiki/Interest_tax "Interest tax") rate from 5% to 3.75%. This move eased the burden on the state's required finances, reduced the fears of state [bondholders](/wiki/Government_debt "Government debt"), and encouraged the growth of [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry"). The subsequent [budget surplus](/wiki/Budget_balance "Budget balance") made it possible to finance major government work projects which massively reduced [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment"), such as the completion of the [Simplon Tunnel](/wiki/Simplon_Tunnel "Simplon Tunnel") in 1906. Shortly after the railway began its triumphal march through [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), each region wanted its own north–south connection and with the construction of the railway tunnels on the [Gotthard](/wiki/Gotthard_Tunnel "Gotthard Tunnel") 14,998 km (1872–1880), [Simplon](/wiki/Simplon_Tunnel "Simplon Tunnel") 19,803 km (1898–1906) and [Lötschberg](/wiki/L%C3%B6tschberg_Tunnel "Lötschberg Tunnel") 14,612 km (1907–1913), three major [Alpine](/wiki/Alps "Alps") crossings were realized that were important for [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and neighboring European countries. The workforce of these monumental projects were largely Italians: at the Gotthard tunnel 90% of miners came from northern Italy, while at the Lötschberg tunnel 97% were Italian, chiefly from the south.

In addition to the now completed nationalization of the railways, the planned nationalization of insurance was tackled and the trade war with France, which had lasted since 1887, ended. Giolitti thereby interrupted Crispi's pro-German foreign policy and thus enabled the export of fruit, vegetables and wine to France. He also boosted the cultivation of [sugar beets](/wiki/Sugar_beet "Sugar beet") and their processing in the [Po Valley](/wiki/Po_Valley "Po Valley") and encouraged heavy industry to gain a foothold in the south as well. However, the latter was not very successful. In 1908, some laws limiting working hours for women and children up to 12 hours were passed with the support of the Socialist MPs.[[36]](#cite_note-Gernert_et_al._2016,_p._369-39) Special laws for the disadvantaged regions of the south followed. However, their implementation mostly failed due to the resistance of the large landowners. Nevertheless, there was a significant improvement in the economic situation of [smallholders](/wiki/Smallholding "Smallholding").

In 1911, 55.4% of the Italian population worked in [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") and 26.9% in [industry](/wiki/Industry_(economics) "Industry (economics)").[[37]](#cite_note-40)

### Social changes and mass emigration

See also: [Caltavuturo massacre](/wiki/Caltavuturo_massacre "Caltavuturo massacre"), [Bava Beccaris massacre](/wiki/Bava_Beccaris_massacre "Bava Beccaris massacre"), and [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Quarto_Stato.jpg/250px-Quarto_Stato.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quarto_Stato.jpg)

*[The Fourth Estate](/wiki/The_Fourth_Estate_(painting) "The Fourth Estate (painting)")* by [Giuseppe Pellizza](/wiki/Giuseppe_Pellizza_da_Volpedo "Giuseppe Pellizza da Volpedo"), c. 1898–1901

Strong social tensions came to light, Italy's social legislation took last place in Europe,[[38]](#cite_note-41) the socialists were opposed not only to social policy but also to colonial expansion. Prime Minister [Francesco Crispi](/wiki/Francesco_Crispi "Francesco Crispi") financed the colonial policy with tax increases and austerity measures. The internal political differences culminated in the [Bava Beccaris massacre](/wiki/Bava_Beccaris_massacre "Bava Beccaris massacre") in Milan. There, on 7 May 1898, there were mass demonstrations against rising bread prices. General [Fiorenzo Bava-Beccaris](/wiki/Fiorenzo_Bava-Beccaris "Fiorenzo Bava-Beccaris"), after the state of siege was declared, fired artillery and rifles at the crowd.[[39]](#cite_note-42) Depending on the information, between 82 and 300 people were killed.[[40]](#cite_note-43)[[41]](#cite_note-44) King [Umberto I](/wiki/Umberto_I_of_Italy "Umberto I of Italy") congratulated the general in a telegram and awarded him a medal. This made him enemies, and in 1900 he, who had been king for 22 years, was shot in [Monza](/wiki/Monza "Monza") by the anarchist [Gaetano Bresci](/wiki/Gaetano_Bresci "Gaetano Bresci").

His successor was [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel III of Italy") politically dominant however was [Giolitti](/wiki/Giovanni_Giolitti "Giovanni Giolitti"), who was initially Minister of the Interior from 1901 to 1903, then prime minister from 1903 with interruptions until 1914 (and often also Minister of the Interior at the same time). He dominated or shaped Italian politics to such an extent that one speaks of the *Giolitti era*. He was willing to make concessions to the reformist and revolutionary movements and promoted industrialization. It is true that state subsidies for private health insurance were introduced in 1886 and the first compulsory accident insurance was introduced in 1898,[[42]](#cite_note-45) but it was Giolitti that introduced state social insurance in 1912 based on the German model. He also reformed the right to vote so that there were no more property limits and the number of eligible voters rose to 8 million men. Unemployment insurance came into being as early as 1919, eight years before Germany.[[43]](#cite_note-46)

In the 1880s there were serious industrial disputes, and around 1889 repression against the Partito Operaio (Labour Party) began, so that the aim was to unite all socialist organizations in the country in one party. The [Fasci Siciliani](/wiki/Fasci_Siciliani "Fasci Siciliani"), short for "Fasci siciliani dei lavoratori", Italian Sicilian Workers' Union was perceived as the "first act of Italian socialism". This popular movement of [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") and [socialist](/wiki/Socialist "Socialist") inspiration that arose in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") in the years between 1892 and 1893, was crushed after harsh military operations in 1894. The industrial workers managed to organize in 1892 in the [Partito dei Lavoratori Italiani](/wiki/Partito_dei_Lavoratori_Italiani "Partito dei Lavoratori Italiani") (Italian Workers' Party), which in 1893 was renamed [Partito Socialista Italiano](/wiki/Partito_Socialista_Italiano "Partito Socialista Italiano") (Italian Socialist Party). Prime Minister [Francesco Crispi](/wiki/Francesco_Crispi "Francesco Crispi") pushed through exceptional laws against the Socialists from 1894, but they were ultimately unsuccessful. In 1901 his successor [Giovanni Giolitti](/wiki/Giovanni_Giolitti "Giovanni Giolitti") tried to integrate the party, which had won 32 seats in the elections, into the government, but the latter refused. But from 1908 to 1912 there was cooperation with the bourgeois left until radical syndicalism prevailed. In 1912 the [Partito Socialista Riformista Italiano](/wiki/Partito_Socialista_Riformista_Italiano "Partito Socialista Riformista Italiano") split off, which for patriotic reasons agreed to the [War against the Ottomans](/wiki/Italian-Turkish_War "Italian-Turkish War"). In 1917, the majority of socialist deputies became pro-war, but the party leadership continued to oppose the war.

Population development of the Kingdom of Italy, by year (1861–1946)[[44]](#cite_note-47)

The state reaction to the drastic social changes came very late, because the social elites, landowners in the south and industrialists in the north, refused for a long time and often relied on the work of the church, which had dominated the social systems since the Middle Ages. However, it was no longer supported by an adequate municipal or [guild system](/wiki/Italian_guilds "Italian guilds"). The population of Italy increased from 18.3 million in 1800 to 24.7 in 1850, finally to 33.8 in 1900.[[45]](#cite_note-48) Nevertheless, Italy's share of the population of Europe continued to fall. On the one hand, this was due to its developmental deficit and, on the other hand, to the fact that from about 1852 there was a large-scale mass emigration. By 1985, around 29 million people had been recorded. From 1876 to around 1890, most came from the north, especially from Venetia (17.9%), Friuli-Venezia Giulia (16.1%) and Piedmont (12.5%). After that, Italians from the south increasingly emigrated. From 1876 to 1915, more than 14 million people emigrated chiefly for [south](/wiki/South_America "South America") and [north](/wiki/North_America "North America") America, of which 8.3 million came from the northern half, including 2.7 million alone from the northeast and from the southern half 5.6 million emigrated.[[46]](#cite_note-49) The main destinations were the United States of America, in which the descendants of the Italians ([Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans")) today represent the third largest European immigrant group after [Germans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") and [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") with a population share of 6%, along with [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") ([Italian Argentines](/wiki/Italian_Argentines "Italian Argentines")), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") ([Italian Brazilians](/wiki/Italian_Brazilians "Italian Brazilians")) and [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") ([Italian Uruguayans](/wiki/Italian_Uruguayans "Italian Uruguayans")). Many also emigrated to [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and other Latin American countries.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/ITALIAN_IMMIGRANTS_%281905%29_ELLIS_ISLAND_NY.png/250px-ITALIAN_IMMIGRANTS_%281905%29_ELLIS_ISLAND_NY.png)](/wiki/File:ITALIAN_IMMIGRANTS_(1905)_ELLIS_ISLAND_NY.png)

Italian immigrants arriving at [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") in 1905

The main reason for emigration was widespread poverty, especially among the rural population. Up until the 1950s, parts of Italy remained a rural, agrarian, and pre-modern society, with agricultural conditions not suitable for keeping farmers in the country, particularly in the northeast and south.[[47]](#cite_note-MacDonald-50) The extent of emigration can be explained on the one hand by the decline of agriculture and the sharp conflicts, which were exacerbated by the preservation of old structures and the lack of capital as well as by large landowners and half-tenancy. At the same time, the hesitant [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") in the fast-growing cities hardly offered enough jobs. In addition, domestic consumption was low, especially since the [fiscalism](/wiki/Fiscalism "Fiscalism") that was believed to be necessary to expand infrastructure continued to weigh on incomes. After all, the companies were equipped with only little capital compared to the foreign ones. Therefore, the government set up high tariff barriers from 1878 to 1887 and pursued a protectionist policy intended to protect the still weak textile and heavy industry in the development phase. France in turn responded to the protective tariff policy with corresponding counter-tariffs.

While [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization") was promoted and [infrastructure](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") expanded in the north, the government in the south supported the [latifundia](/wiki/Latifundia "Latifundia"), whereby in both cases the protagonists of heavy industry and agriculture were able to assert their influence in the north and south. In central Italy there was a different system for the peasants. Land could be leased here and they could keep a relatively large amount, so there was less migration from this part of the country than from other parts. There was less migration from large cities, but there was a major exception to this. [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") was the capital of the [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples") and later of the [Kingdom of the Two Sicilies](/wiki/Kingdom_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Kingdom of the Two Sicilies") for six hundred years and in 1861 became simply a city in the united Italy. As a result, many bureaucratic jobs were lost and there was a lot of unemployment. Due to a [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera") epidemic in the 1880s, many people also decided to leave the city. In the south the unification abolished the feudal system that had survived since the [middle ages](/wiki/Middle_ages "Middle ages"). However, this did not mean that the farmers now got their own land that they could work on. Many remained without property and plots became smaller and smaller and thus more unproductive after lands were divided among heirs. Another reason was the [overpopulation](/wiki/Overpopulation "Overpopulation"), especially in the [south](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") (*Mezzogiorno*). After unification southern Italy established access to running water and medical care in hospitals for the first time. This reduced infant mortality and, together with what had been the highest birth rate in Europe for a long time, led to an increase in population, which in turn forced many young southern Italians to emigrate at the beginning of the 20th century.

Currency policy caused major problems, because during the Franco-Prussian War Italy also suspended free [convertibility](/wiki/Convertibility "Convertibility"). Now the [gold standard](/wiki/Gold_standard "Gold standard") prevailed, which ensured that banknotes could only be issued in a fixed proportion to the [gold reserves](/wiki/Gold_reserve "Gold reserve"). It was expected that this would stabilize currency relations through the gold automatism, whereby the respective [central banks](/wiki/Central_banks "Central banks") had to adhere to strict rules. If a [currency](/wiki/Currency "Currency") became weaker, this led to a gold outflow in the direction of the stronger currency, with the result that the banknote issue had to be reduced in line with the reduced gold reserves. This raised interest rates and lowered prices. In contrast, in the country where gold was flocking, this created more paper money in circulation, lowering interest rates and raising prices. At a certain point, the flow of gold reversed, the [balance of payments](/wiki/Balance_of_payments "Balance of payments") settled, and the currency stabilized. Even if the central banks often did not comply with the guidelines, the system was successful because people trusted that money and gold could be exchanged at any time. By linking the [Latin Monetary Union](/wiki/Latin_Monetary_Union "Latin Monetary Union"), founded in 1865 and based on bimetallism, i.e. gold and silver coins, and thus the [lira](/wiki/Italian_lira "Italian lira") to gold, the government was able to create so much trust that foreign investment capital after Italy came. Treasury Secretary [Sidney Sonnino](/wiki/Sidney_Sonnino "Sidney Sonnino") also tried to put a strain on large fortunes in the same way as consumption was put under pressure, but he failed due to conservative opposition. With the overcoming of the economic crisis from 1896, it was nevertheless possible to achieve a balanced budget.

#### Southern question and Italian diaspora

See also: [Southern question](/wiki/Southern_question "Southern question"), [Brigandage in Southern Italy after 1861](/wiki/Brigandage_in_Southern_Italy_after_1861 "Brigandage in Southern Italy after 1861"), and [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Briganti_lucani_Caruso_Cafo_Lamacchia_e_Tinna.jpg/250px-Briganti_lucani_Caruso_Cafo_Lamacchia_e_Tinna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Briganti_lucani_Caruso_Cafo_Lamacchia_e_Tinna.jpg)

Some Lucanian brigands: [Caruso](/wiki/Giuseppe_Caruso_(brigand) "Giuseppe Caruso (brigand)"), Cafo, Lamacchia and Tinna

Italy's population remained severely divided between wealthy elites and impoverished workers, especially in the South. An 1881 census found that over 1 million southern day-laborers were chronically under-employed and likely to become seasonal emigrants to sustain themselves economically.[[48]](#cite_note-Clark_7-51) Southern peasants, as well as small landowners and tenants, often were in a state of conflict and revolt throughout the late 19th century.[[49]](#cite_note-52) There were exceptions to the generally poor economic condition of agricultural workers of the South, as some regions near cities such as [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") and [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo") as well as along the [Tyrrhenian Sea](/wiki/Tyrrhenian_Sea "Tyrrhenian Sea") coast.[[48]](#cite_note-Clark_7-51) From the 1870s onward, intellectuals, scholars and politicians examined the economic and social conditions of [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy") (*Il Mezzogiorno*), a movement known as *Meridionalismo* ("Meridionalism"). For example, the *1910 Commission of Inquiry into the South* indicated that the Italian government thus far had failed to ameliorate the severe economic differences. The limited voting rights only to those with sufficient property allowed rich landowners to exploit the poor.[[50]](#cite_note-53) This situation of persistent backwardness in the socioeconomic development of the regions of southern Italy compared to the other regions of the country, especially the [northern ones](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy"), is called [Southern question](/wiki/Southern_question "Southern question").

The transition from a peninsula divided into several states to a unified Italy was not smooth for the south (the *[Mezzogiorno](/wiki/Mezzogiorno "Mezzogiorno")*). The path to unification and modernization created a divide between Northern and Southern Italy. People condemned the South for being "backwards" and barbaric, when in truth, compared to Northern Italy, "where there was backwardness, the lag, never excessive, was always more or less compensated by other elements".[[51]](#cite_note-54) The entire region south of Naples was afflicted with numerous deep economic and social liabilities.[[52]](#cite_note-55) However, many of the South's political problems and its reputation of being "passive" or lazy (politically speaking) was due to the new government (that was born out of Italy's want for development) that alienated the South and prevented the people of the South from any say in important matters. However, on the other hand, transportation was difficult, soil fertility was low with extensive erosion, deforestation was severe, many businesses could stay open only because of high protective tariffs, large estates were often poorly managed, most peasants had only very small plots, and there was chronic unemployment and high crime rates.[[53]](#cite_note-Roland_Sarti_2004_pp_567-56)

Cavour decided the basic problem was poor government, and believed that could be remedied by strict application of the Piedmonese legal system. The main result was an upsurge in [brigandage](/wiki/Brigandage_in_the_Two_Sicilies "Brigandage in the Two Sicilies"), which turned into a bloody civil war that lasted almost ten years. The insurrection reached its peak mainly in [Basilicata](/wiki/Basilicata "Basilicata") and northern [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"), headed by the brigands [Carmine Crocco](/wiki/Carmine_Crocco "Carmine Crocco") and Michele Caruso.[[54]](#cite_note-57) With the end of the southern riots, there was a heavy outflow of millions of peasants in the [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora"), especially to the United States and South America. Others relocated to the northern industrial cities such as Genoa, Milan and Turin, and sent money home.[[53]](#cite_note-Roland_Sarti_2004_pp_567-56)

The first Italian diaspora began around 1880, two decades after the [Unification of Italy](/wiki/Risorgimento "Risorgimento"), and ended in the 1920s to the early 1940s with the rise of [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_under_Fascism_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Kingdom of Italy under Fascism (1922–1943)").[[55]](#cite_note-Pozzetta,_George_E._1992-58) Poverty was the main reason for emigration, specifically the lack of land as *[mezzadria](/wiki/Mezzadria "Mezzadria")* [sharecropping](/wiki/Sharecropping "Sharecropping") flourished in Italy, especially in the South, and property became subdivided over generations. Especially in [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"), conditions were harsh.[[55]](#cite_note-Pozzetta,_George_E._1992-58) Until the 1860s to 1950s, most of Italy was a [rural society](/wiki/Rural_society "Rural society") with many small towns and cities and almost no modern industry in which land management practices, especially in the South and the [northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_Italy "Northeastern Italy"), did not easily convince farmers to stay on the land and to work the soil.[[47]](#cite_note-MacDonald-50)

Another factor was related to the overpopulation of Southern Italy as a result of the improvements in socioeconomic conditions after [Unification](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy").[[56]](#cite_note-59) That created a demographic boom and forced the new generations to emigrate en masse in the late 19th century and the early 20th century, mostly to the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").[[57]](#cite_note-Italy's_Many_Diasporas-60) The new migration of capital created millions of unskilled jobs around the world and was responsible for the simultaneous mass migration of Italians searching for "work and bread" ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *pane e lavoro*, Italian: [[ˈpaːne e llaˈvoːro]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian")).[[58]](#cite_note-61)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/2016_-_Vittoriano_%28Rome%29_07.jpg/250px-2016_-_Vittoriano_%28Rome%29_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_-_Vittoriano_(Rome)_07.jpg)

One of the two braziers that burn perpetually on the sides of the [tomb of the Italian Unknown Soldier](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Unknown_Soldier_(Italy) "Tomb of the Unknown Soldier (Italy)") at [Altare della Patria](/wiki/Altare_della_Patria "Altare della Patria") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). At their base there is a plaque bearing the inscription *Gli italiani all'estero alla Madre Patria* ("Italians abroad to the Motherland").

The [Unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy") broke down the feudal land system, which had survived in the south since the Middle Ages, especially where land had been the inalienable property of aristocrats, religious bodies or the king. The breakdown of [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism"), however, and redistribution of land did not necessarily lead to small farmers in the south winding up with land of their own or land they could work and make profit from. Many remained landless, and plots grew smaller and smaller and so less and less productive, as land was subdivided amongst heirs.[[47]](#cite_note-MacDonald-50)

Between 1860 and World War I, 9 million Italians left permanently of a total of 16 million who emigrated, most travelling to North or South America.[[59]](#cite_note-Hatton-62) The numbers may have even been higher; 14 million from 1876 to 1914, according to another study. Annual emigration averaged almost 220,000 in the period 1876 to 1900, and almost 650,000 from 1901 through 1915. Prior to 1900 the majority of Italian immigrants were from northern and central Italy.
