Two-thirds of the migrants who left Italy between 1870 and 1914 were men with traditional skills. Peasants were half of all migrants before 1896.[[57]](#cite_note-Italy's_Many_Diasporas-60)

The bond of the emigrants with their mother country continued to be very strong even after their departure. Many Italian emigrants made donations to the construction of the [Altare della Patria](/wiki/Altare_della_Patria "Altare della Patria") (1885–1935), a part of the monument dedicated to King [Victor Emmanuel II of Italy](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel II of Italy"), and in memory of that, the inscription of the plaque on the two burning braziers perpetually at the Altare della Patria next to the [tomb of the Italian Unknown Soldier](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_Unknown_Soldier_(Italy) "Tomb of the Unknown Soldier (Italy)"), reads *Gli italiani all'estero alla Madre Patria* ("Italians abroad to the Motherland").[[60]](#cite_note-63) The [allegorical](/wiki/Allegory "Allegory") meaning of the flames that burn perpetually is linked to their symbolism, which is centuries old, since it has its origins in [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"), especially in the [cult of the dead](/wiki/Funeral "Funeral").[[61]](#cite_note-fiamma-64) A fire that burns eternally symbolizes that the memory, in this case of the sacrifice of the Unknown Soldier and the [bond of the country of origin](/wiki/Fatherland "Fatherland"), is perpetually alive in Italians, even in those who are far from their country, and will never fade.[[61]](#cite_note-fiamma-64)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Italy](/wiki/Education_in_Italy "Education in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Literacy_rates_in_Italy_1861.svg/330px-Literacy_rates_in_Italy_1861.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Literacy_rates_in_Italy_1861.svg)

Literacy rates in Italy in 1861, shortly after the [proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy"). Italy lacks [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio") and the [Triveneto](/wiki/Triveneto "Triveneto"), which were subsequently annexed.

In Italy a state school system or education system has existed since 1859, when the *Legge Casati* (Casati Act) mandated educational responsibilities for the forthcoming Italian state ([Italian unification](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") took place in 1861).

The Casati Act made primary education (*scuola elementare*) compulsory, and had the goal of increasing [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy"). This law gave control of primary education to the single towns, of secondary education to the [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Italy "Provinces of Italy"), and the universities were managed by the State. Even with the Casati Act and compulsory education, in rural (and southern) areas children often were not sent to school (the rate of children enrolled in primary education would reach 90% only after 70 years) and the illiteracy rate (which was nearly 80% in 1861) took more than 50 years to halve.

The next important law concerning the Italian education system was the *[Legge Gentile](/wiki/Legge_Gentile "Legge Gentile")*. This act was issued in 1923, thus when [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") and his [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party") were in power. In fact, [Giovanni Gentile](/wiki/Giovanni_Gentile "Giovanni Gentile") was appointed the task of creating an education system deemed fit for the fascist system. The compulsory age of education was raised to 14 years, and was somewhat based on a ladder system: after the first five years of primary education, one could choose the *scuola media*, which would give further access to the *liceo* and other secondary education, or the 'avviamento al lavoro' (work training), which was intended to give a quick entry into the low strates of the workforce. The reform enhanced the role of the *Liceo Classico*, created by the Casati Act in 1859 (and intended during the Fascist era as the peak of secondary education, with the goal of forming the future upper classes), and created the Technical, Commercial and Industrial institutes and the *Liceo Scientifico*. The influence of Gentile's [Italian idealism](/wiki/Italian_idealism "Italian idealism") was great,[[62]](#cite_note-65) and he considered the Catholic religion to be the "foundation and crowning" of education.

### Liberal era of politics (1870–1914)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Galleria_Milano_%28179532365%29.jpeg/250px-Galleria_Milano_%28179532365%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Galleria_Milano_(179532365).jpeg)

[Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II](/wiki/Galleria_Vittorio_Emanuele_II "Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II") in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") was an architectural work created by [Giuseppe Mengoni](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mengoni "Giuseppe Mengoni") between 1865 and 1877 and named after the first king of unified Italy, [Victor Emmanuel II](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_II "Victor Emmanuel II").

After unification, Italy's politics favored [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism"):[[a]](#cite_note-66) the liberal-conservative right (*destra storica* or Historical Right) was regionally fragmented[[b]](#cite_note-67) and liberal-conservative prime minister [Marco Minghetti](/wiki/Marco_Minghetti "Marco Minghetti") only held on to power by enacting revolutionary and left-leaning policies (such as the nationalization of railways) to appease the opposition.

#### Agostino Depretis

In 1876, Minghetti was ousted and replaced by liberal [Agostino Depretis](/wiki/Agostino_Depretis "Agostino Depretis"), who began the long Liberal Period. The Liberal Period was marked by corruption, government instability, continued poverty in Southern Italy and the use of authoritarian measures by the Italian government.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/1b/Agostino_Depretis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Agostino_Depretis.jpg)

[Agostino Depretis](/wiki/Agostino_Depretis "Agostino Depretis")

Depretis began his term as prime minister by initiating an experimental political notion known as *[trasformismo](/wiki/Trasformismo "Trasformismo")* ("transformism"). The theory of *trasformismo* was that a cabinet should select a variety of moderates and capable politicians from a non-partisan perspective. In practice, *trasformismo* was authoritarian and corrupt as Depretis pressured districts to vote for his candidates if they wished to gain favourable concessions from Depretis when in power. The results of the Italian general election of 1876 resulted in only four representatives from the right being elected, allowing the government to be dominated by Depretis. Despotic and corrupt actions are believed to be the key means by which Depretis managed to keep support in Southern Italy. Depretis put through authoritarian measures, such as banning public meetings, placing "dangerous" individuals in internal exile on remote penal islands across Italy and adopting militarist policies. Depretis enacted controversial legislation for the time, such as abolishing arrest for debt and making elementary education free and compulsory while ending compulsory religious teaching in elementary schools.[[63]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Triple_Alliance.png/500px-Triple_Alliance.png)](/wiki/File:Triple_Alliance.png)

The [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") in 1913, shown in red

In 1887, [Francesco Crispi](/wiki/Francesco_Crispi "Francesco Crispi") became prime minister and began focusing government efforts on foreign policy. Crispi worked to build Italy as a great world power through increased military expenditures, advocacy of expansionism[[64]](#cite_note-69) and trying to win the favor of [Germany](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"). Italy joined the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)"), which included both Germany and [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") in 1882 and which remained officially intact until 1915. While helping Italy develop strategically, he continued *trasformismo* and became authoritarian, once suggesting the use of martial law to ban opposition parties.[[65]](#cite_note-70) Despite being authoritarian, Crispi put through liberal policies such as the Public Health Act of 1888 and established tribunals for redress against abuses by the government.[[66]](#cite_note-71)

#### Francesco Crispi

[Francesco Crispi](/wiki/Francesco_Crispi "Francesco Crispi") was prime minister for a total of six years, from 1887 until 1891 and again from 1893 until 1896. Historian R. J. B. Bosworth says of his foreign policy:

> Crispi pursued policies whose openly aggressive character would not be equaled until the days of the Fascist regime. Crispi increased military expenditure, talked cheerfully of a European conflagration, and alarmed his German or British friends with signs of preventative attacks on his enemies. His policies were ruinous for Italy's trade with France and, more humiliatingly, for colonial ambitions in Eastern Africa. Crispi's lust for territory there was thwarted when on 1 March 1896, the armies of Ethiopian Emperor Menelik routed Italian forces at Adowa [...] an unparalleled disaster for a modern army. Crispi, whose private life (he was perhaps a trigamist) and personal finances [...] were objects of perennial scandal, went into dishonorable retirement.[[67]](#cite_note-72)

Crispi greatly admired the United Kingdom, but was unable to get British assistance for his aggressive foreign policy and turned instead to Germany.[[68]](#cite_note-73) Crispi also enlarged the army and navy and advocated expansionism as he sought Germany's favor by joining the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") which included both Germany and Austria-Hungary in 1882. It remained officially intact until 1915 and prevented hostilities between Italy and Austria, which controlled border regions that Italy claimed.

#### Colonialism

Main article: [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Francesco_Crispi_crop.jpg/250px-Francesco_Crispi_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francesco_Crispi_crop.jpg)

[Francesco Crispi](/wiki/Francesco_Crispi "Francesco Crispi") promoted the Italian colonialism in Africa in the late 19th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Italian_Empire_maximum_extent_1942-43.png/250px-Italian_Empire_maximum_extent_1942-43.png)](/wiki/File:Italian_Empire_maximum_extent_1942-43.png)

[Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire") at greatest extent in 1942-43.

In the late 19th and early 20th century, Italy emulated the Great Powers in acquiring colonies, especially in the scramble to take control of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") that took place in the 1870s. Italy was weak in military and economic resources compared to Britain, France and Germany. Still, it proved difficult due to popular resistance. It was unprofitable due to high military costs and the lesser economic value of spheres of influence remaining when Italy began to colonize. Britain was eager to block French influence and assisted Italy in gaining territory of the Red Sea.[[69]](#cite_note-74)

Several colonial projects were undertaken by the government. These were done to gain the support of Italian nationalists and imperialists, who wanted to rebuild a Roman Empire. Italy had already large settlements in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") and [Tunis](/wiki/Tunis "Tunis"). Italy first attempted to gain colonies through negotiations with other world powers to make colonial concessions, but these negotiations failed. Italy also sent missionaries to uncolonized lands to investigate the potential for Italian colonization. The most promising and realistic of these were parts of Africa. Italian missionaries had already established a foothold at [Massawa](/wiki/Massawa "Massawa") (in present-day [Eritrea](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea")) in the 1830s and had entered deep into the [Ethiopian Empire](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire").[[70]](#cite_note-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Massaua_1886._Veduta_del_campo_Gherar._Soldati_de_Genio_Ferrovieri_fra_le_rotaie_che_avrebbero_collegato_Massaua_a_Saati_%28Foto_Ledru_e_Nicorta%29.jpg/250px-Massaua_1886._Veduta_del_campo_Gherar._Soldati_de_Genio_Ferrovieri_fra_le_rotaie_che_avrebbero_collegato_Massaua_a_Saati_%28Foto_Ledru_e_Nicorta%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Massaua_1886._Veduta_del_campo_Gherar._Soldati_de_Genio_Ferrovieri_fra_le_rotaie_che_avrebbero_collegato_Massaua_a_Saati_(Foto_Ledru_e_Nicorta).jpg)

Members of the [Ferrovieri Engineer Regiment](/wiki/Ferrovieri_Engineer_Regiment "Ferrovieri Engineer Regiment") during the construction of the rails to connect [Massawa](/wiki/Massawa "Massawa") to Saati in [Italian Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea"), 1886.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Cartolina_Giovanni_Pastorelli.jpg/250px-Cartolina_Giovanni_Pastorelli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cartolina_Giovanni_Pastorelli.jpg)

The [Ain Zara oasis](/wiki/Battle_of_Ain_Zara "Battle of Ain Zara") during the [Italo-Turkish War](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War"): propaganda postcard made by the Italian Army

The beginning of colonialism came in 1885, shortly after the fall of [Egyptian rule](/wiki/History_of_Egypt_under_the_Muhammad_Ali_dynasty "History of Egypt under the Muhammad Ali dynasty") in [Khartoum](/wiki/Khartoum "Khartoum"), when Italy landed soldiers at [Massawa](/wiki/Massawa "Massawa") in East Africa. In 1888, Italy annexed Massawa by force, creating the colony of [Italian Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea"). The Eritrean ports of Massawa and Assab handled trade with Italy and Ethiopia. The trade was promoted by the low duties paid on Italian trade. Italy exported manufactured products and imported coffee, beeswax and hides.[[71]](#cite_note-76) At the same time, Italy occupied territory on the south side of the horn of Africa, forming what would become [Italian Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland").

The [Treaty of Wuchale](/wiki/Treaty_of_Wuchale "Treaty of Wuchale"), signed in 1889, stated in the Italian language version that Ethiopia was to become an Italian protectorate, while the Ethiopian Amharic language version stated that the Ethiopian Emperor [Menelik II](/wiki/Menelik_II_of_Ethiopia "Menelik II of Ethiopia") could go through Italy to conduct foreign affairs. This happened presumably due to the mistranslation of a verb, which formed a permissive clause in Amharic and a mandatory one in Italian.[[72]](#cite_note-77) When the differences in the versions came to light, in 1895 Menelik II abrogated the treaty and abandoned the agreement to follow Italian foreign policy.[[73]](#cite_note-78) Because of the Ethiopian refusal to abide by the Italian version of the treaty and despite economic handicaps at home, the Italian government decided on a military solution to force Ethiopia to abide by the Italian version of the treaty. In doing so, they believed that they could exploit divisions within Ethiopia and rely on tactical and technological superiority to offset any inferiority in numbers. As a result, Italy and Ethiopia came into confrontation, in what was later to be known as the [First Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/First_Italo-Ethiopian_War "First Italo-Ethiopian War").[[74]](#cite_note-79)

The Italian army failed on the battlefield and was overwhelmed by a huge Ethiopian army at the [Battle of Adwa](/wiki/Battle_of_Adwa "Battle of Adwa"). At that point, the Italian invasion force was forced to retreat into Eritrea. The war formally ended with the [Treaty of Addis Ababa](/wiki/Treaty_of_Addis_Ababa "Treaty of Addis Ababa") in 1896, which abrogated the Treaty of Wuchale, recognizing Ethiopia as an independent country. The failed Ethiopian campaign was one of the few military victories scored by the Africans against an imperial power at this time.[[75]](#cite_note-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Italian_mounted_infantry_in_China_1900_HD-SN-99-01989.jpg/250px-Italian_mounted_infantry_in_China_1900_HD-SN-99-01989.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_mounted_infantry_in_China_1900_HD-SN-99-01989.jpg)

Italian mounted infantry in [China](/wiki/Qing_Dynasty "Qing Dynasty") during the [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") in 1900

From 2 November 1899 to 7 September 1901, Italy participated as part of the [Eight-Nation Alliance](/wiki/Eight-Nation_Alliance "Eight-Nation Alliance") forces during the [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") in [China](/wiki/China "China"). On 7 September 1901, a concession in [Tientsin](/wiki/Tianjin "Tianjin") was ceded to Italy by the [Qing Dynasty](/wiki/Qing_Dynasty "Qing Dynasty"). On 7 June 1902, the concession was taken into Italian possession and administered by an Italian [consul](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)").

In 1911, Italy declared war on the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") and [invaded](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War") [Tripolitania](/wiki/Tripolitania "Tripolitania"), [Fezzan](/wiki/Fezzan "Fezzan") and [Cyrenaica](/wiki/Cyrenaica "Cyrenaica"). These provinces together formed what became known as [Libya](/wiki/Italian_Libya "Italian Libya"). The war ended only one year later, but the occupation resulted in acts of discrimination against Libyans, such as the forced deportation of Libyans to the [Tremiti Islands](/wiki/Tremiti_Islands "Tremiti Islands") in October 1911. By 1912, one-third of these Libyan refugees had died from a lack of food and shelter.[[76]](#cite_note-81) The annexation of Libya led nationalists to advocate Italian domination of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") by occupying [Greece](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Greece "Kingdom of Greece") and the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") coastal region of [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia").[[77]](#cite_note-82)

#### Giovanni Giolitti

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/04/Giovanni_Giolitti_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giovanni_Giolitti_2.jpg)

[Giovanni Giolitti](/wiki/Giovanni_Giolitti "Giovanni Giolitti") was [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") five times between 1892 and 1921.

In 1892, [Giovanni Giolitti](/wiki/Giovanni_Giolitti "Giovanni Giolitti") became Prime Minister of Italy for his first term. Although his first government quickly collapsed one year later, Giolitti returned in 1903 to lead Italy's government during a fragmented period until 1914. Giolitti had spent his earlier life as a civil servant and then took positions within the cabinets of Crispi. Giolitti was the first long-term Italian Prime Minister because he mastered the political concept of *[trasformismo](/wiki/Trasformismo "Trasformismo")* by manipulating, coercing and bribing officials to his side. In elections during Giolitti's government, voting fraud was common. Giolitti helped improve voting only in well-off, more supportive areas while attempting to isolate and intimidate poor areas where opposition was strong.[[78]](#cite_note-83) Southern Italy was in terrible shape before and during Giolitti's tenure as Prime Minister: four-fifths of southern Italians were illiterate, and the dire situation there ranged from problems of large numbers of absentee landlords to rebellion and even starvation.[[79]](#cite_note-84) Corruption was such a large problem that Giolitti himself admitted that there were places "where the law does not operate at all".[[80]](#cite_note-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Zeplin_orta.jpg/250px-Zeplin_orta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zeplin_orta.jpg)

Italian dirigibles bomb Turkish positions in Libya, as the [Italo-Turkish War](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War") of 1911–1912 was the first in history in which air attacks (carried out here by dirigible airships) determined the outcome.

In 1911, Giolitti's government sent forces to occupy Libya. While the success of the [Libyan War](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Libya "Italian invasion of Libya") improved the status of the nationalists, it did not help Giolitti's administration as a whole. The government attempted to discourage criticism by speaking about Italy's strategic achievements and inventiveness of their military in the war: Italy was the [first country](/wiki/Giulio_Gavotti "Giulio Gavotti") to use the [airship](/wiki/Airship "Airship") for military purposes and undertook [aerial bombing](/wiki/Strategic_bombing "Strategic bombing") on the Ottoman forces.[[81]](#cite_note-86) The war radicalized the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party"), and anti-war revolutionaries called for violence to bring down the government. [Elections were held in 1913](/wiki/1913_Italian_general_election "1913 Italian general election"), and Giolitti's coalition retained an absolute majority in the Chamber of Deputies, while the [Radical Party](/wiki/Italian_Radical_Party "Italian Radical Party") emerged as the largest opposition bloc. The Italian Socialist Party gained eight seats and was the largest party in [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna").[[82]](#cite_note-Corbetta-87) Giolitti's coalition did not endure long after the election, and he was forced to resign in March 1914. Giolitti later returned as Prime Minister only briefly in 1920, but the era of liberalism was effectively over in Italy.

The [1913](/wiki/1913_Italian_general_election "1913 Italian general election") and [1919](/wiki/1919_Italian_general_election "1919 Italian general election") elections saw gains made by Socialist, Catholic and nationalist parties at the expense of the traditionally dominant Liberals and [Radicals](/wiki/Italian_Radical_Party "Italian Radical Party"), who were increasingly fractured and weakened as a result.

### World War I and failure of the liberal state (1915–1922)

See also: [Italian entry into World War I](/wiki/Italian_entry_into_World_War_I "Italian entry into World War I"), [Italian Front (World War I)](/wiki/Italian_Front_(World_War_I) "Italian Front (World War I)"), [Military history of Italy during World War I](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_I "Military history of Italy during World War I"), and [Italian Campaign (World War I)](/wiki/Italian_Campaign_(World_War_I) "Italian Campaign (World War I)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg/250px-Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trento_3_novembre_1918.jpg)

Italian cavalry in [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento") on 3 November 1918, after the victorious [Battle of Vittorio Veneto](/wiki/Battle_of_Vittorio_Veneto "Battle of Vittorio Veneto")

Italy entered into the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War") in 1915 with the aim of completing national unity: for this reason, the Italian intervention in the First World War is also considered the [Fourth Italian War of Independence](/wiki/Fourth_Italian_War_of_Independence "Fourth Italian War of Independence"),[[83]](#cite_note-88) in a historiographical perspective that identifies in the latter the conclusion of the [unification of Italy](/wiki/Unification_of_Italy "Unification of Italy").[[84]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-89)[[85]](#cite_note-90)

The war forced the decision whether to honor the alliance with Germany and Austria. For six months Italy remained neutral, as the [Triple Alliance](/wiki/Triple_Alliance_(1882) "Triple Alliance (1882)") was only for defensive purposes. Italy took the initiative in entering the war in spring 1915, despite strong popular and elite sentiment in favor of neutrality. Italy was a large, poor country whose political system was chaotic, its finances were heavily strained, and its army was very poorly prepared.[[86]](#cite_note-91) The Triple Alliance meant little either to Italians or Austrians – Vienna had declared war on Serbia without consulting Rome. Prime Minister [Antonio Salandra](/wiki/Antonio_Salandra "Antonio Salandra") and Foreign Minister [Sidney Sonnino](/wiki/Sidney_Sonnino "Sidney Sonnino") negotiated with both sides in secret for the best deal, and got one from the Entente, which was quite willing to promise large slices of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, including the [Tyrol](/wiki/History_of_Tyrol "History of Tyrol") and [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"), as well as making [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") a protectorate. Russia vetoed giving Italy [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"). Britain was willing to pay subsidies and loans to get 36 million Italians as new allies who threatened the southern flank of Austria.[[87]](#cite_note-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Liberazione_di_Trieste.jpg/250px-Liberazione_di_Trieste.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberazione_di_Trieste.jpg)

Italian troops landing in [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste"), 3 November 1918

When the [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1915) "Treaty of London (1915)") was announced in May 1915, there was an uproar from antiwar elements. Reports from around Italy showed the people feared war, and cared little about territorial gains. Rural folk saw war as a disaster, like drought, famine or plague. Businessmen were generally opposed, fearing heavy-handed government controls and taxes, and loss of foreign markets. Reversing the decision seemed impossible, for the Triple Alliance did not want Italy back, and the king's throne was at risk. Pro-war supporters mobbed the streets. The fervor for war represented a bitterly hostile reaction against politics as usual, and the failures, frustrations, and stupidities of the ruling class.[[88]](#cite_note-93)[[89]](#cite_note-94) [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") created the newspaper *[Il Popolo d'Italia](/wiki/Il_Popolo_d%27Italia "Il Popolo d'Italia")*, which at first attempted to convince socialists and revolutionaries to support the war.[[90]](#cite_note-95) The [Allied Powers](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"), eager to draw Italy to the war, helped finance the newspaper.[[91]](#cite_note-96) Later, after the war, this publication would become the official newspaper of the Fascist movement.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Sacrario_militare_di_Redipuglia_agosto_2014.JPG/250px-Sacrario_militare_di_Redipuglia_agosto_2014.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sacrario_militare_di_Redipuglia_agosto_2014.JPG)

The [Redipuglia War Memorial](/wiki/Redipuglia_War_Memorial "Redipuglia War Memorial") of [Redipuglia](/wiki/Fogliano_Redipuglia "Fogliano Redipuglia"), with the tomb of [Prince Emanuele Filiberto, Duke of Aosta](/wiki/Prince_Emanuele_Filiberto,_Duke_of_Aosta "Prince Emanuele Filiberto, Duke of Aosta") in the foreground

Italy entered the war with an army of 875,000 men, but the army was poorly led and lacked heavy artillery and machine guns, their war supplies having been largely depleted in [the war of 1911–12](/wiki/Italo-Turkish_War "Italo-Turkish War") against Turkey. Italy proved unable to prosecute the war effectively, as fighting raged for three years on a very narrow front along the [Isonzo River](/wiki/Battles_of_the_Isonzo "Battles of the Isonzo"), where the Austrians held the high ground. In 1916, Italy declared war on Germany, which provided significant aid to the Austrians. Some 650,000 Italian soldiers died and 950,000 were wounded, while the economy required large-scale Allied funding to survive.[[92]](#cite_note-97)[[93]](#cite_note-98)

Before the war the government had ignored labor issues, but now it had to intervene to mobilize war production. With the main working-class Socialist party reluctant to support the war effort, strikes were frequent and cooperation was minimal, especially in the Socialist strongholds of Piedmont and Lombardy. The government imposed high wage scales, as well as collective bargaining and insurance schemes.[[94]](#cite_note-99) Many large firms expanded dramatically. Inflation doubled the cost of living. Industrial wages kept pace but not wages for farm workers. Discontent was high in rural areas since so many men were taken for service, industrial jobs were unavailable, wages grew slowly and inflation was just as bad.[[95]](#cite_note-100)

The Italian victory,[[96]](#cite_note-101)[[97]](#cite_note-102)[[98]](#cite_note-103) which was announced by the *[Bollettino della Vittoria](/wiki/Bollettino_della_Vittoria "Bollettino della Vittoria")* and the *[Bollettino della Vittoria Navale](/wiki/Bollettino_della_Vittoria_Navale "Bollettino della Vittoria Navale")*, marked the end of the war on the Italian Front, secured the dissolution of the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire") and was chiefly instrumental in [ending](/wiki/Armistice_with_Germany "Armistice with Germany") the First World War less than two weeks later. More than 651,000 Italian soldiers died on the battlefields.[[99]](#cite_note-Mortara-104) The Italian civilian deaths were estimated at 589,000 due to malnutrition and food shortages.[[100]](#cite_note-105) In November 1918, after the surrender of Austria-Hungary, Italy occupied militarily [Trentino Alto-Adige](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige/S%C3%BCdtirol "Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol"), the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March"), [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria"), the [Kvarner Gulf](/wiki/Kvarner_Gulf "Kvarner Gulf") and [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"), all Austro-Hungarian territories. On the Dalmatian coast, Italy established the [Governorate of Dalmatia](/wiki/Governorate_of_Dalmatia#The_first_Governorate_of_Dalmatia "Governorate of Dalmatia"), which had the provisional aim of ferrying the territory towards full integration into the Kingdom of Italy, progressively importing national legislation in place of the previous one. The administrative capital was [Zara](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar"). The Governorate of Dalmatia was evacuated following the Italo-Yugoslav agreements which resulted in the [Treaty of Rapallo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rapallo_(1920) "Treaty of Rapallo (1920)") (1920).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Promised_Borders_of_the_Tready_of_London.png/250px-Promised_Borders_of_the_Tready_of_London.png)](/wiki/File:Promised_Borders_of_the_Tready_of_London.png)

Territories promised to Italy by the [Treaty of London (1915)](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1915) "Treaty of London (1915)"), i.e. [Trentino-Alto Adige](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige "Trentino-Alto Adige"), the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March") and [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") (tan), and the [Snežnik Plateau](/wiki/Sne%C5%BEnik_(plateau) "Snežnik (plateau)") area (green). Dalmatia, after WWI, however, was not assigned to Italy but to [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia").

As the war came to an end, [Italian Prime Minister](/wiki/Italian_Prime_Minister "Italian Prime Minister") [Vittorio Emanuele Orlando](/wiki/Vittorio_Emanuele_Orlando "Vittorio Emanuele Orlando") met with [British Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), [Prime Minister of France](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_France "Prime Minister of France") [Georges Clemenceau](/wiki/Georges_Clemenceau "Georges Clemenceau") and [United States President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") in [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles") to discuss how the borders of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") should be redefined to help avoid a future European war. The talks provided little territorial gain to Italy as Wilson promised freedom to all European nationalities to form their nation-states. As a result, the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") did not assign [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") and [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania") to Italy as had been promised in the [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London_(1915) "Treaty of London (1915)"). Furthermore, the British and French decided to divide the German overseas colonies into their mandates, with Italy receiving none. Italy also gained no territory from the breakup of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Despite this, Orlando agreed to sign the Treaty of Versailles, which caused uproar against his government. The [Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)") (1919) and the [Treaty of Rapallo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rapallo_(1920) "Treaty of Rapallo (1920)") (1920) allowed the annexation of [Trentino Alto-Adige](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige/S%C3%BCdtirol "Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol"), the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March"), [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria"), [Kvarner](/wiki/Kvarner_Gulf "Kvarner Gulf") as well as the [Dalmatian](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") city of [Zara](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar").

Furious over the peace settlement, the Italian nationalist poet [Gabriele D'Annunzio](/wiki/Gabriele_D%27Annunzio "Gabriele D'Annunzio") led disaffected war veterans and nationalists to form the [Free State of Fiume](/wiki/Free_State_of_Fiume "Free State of Fiume") in September 1919. His popularity among nationalists led him to be called *[Il Duce](/wiki/Il_Duce "Il Duce")* ("The Leader"), and he used black-shirted paramilitary in his assault on Fiume. The leadership title of *Duce* and the blackshirt paramilitary uniform would later be adopted by the [Fascist](/wiki/Italian_Fascism "Italian Fascism") movement of [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"). The demand for the Italian annexation of Fiume spread to all sides of the political spectrum, including Mussolini's Fascists.[[101]](#cite_note-106)

The subsequent [Treaty of Rome](/wiki/Treaty_of_Rome_(1924) "Treaty of Rome (1924)") (1924) led to the annexation of the city of [Fiume](/wiki/Fiume "Fiume") to Italy. Italy's lack of territorial gain led to the outcome being denounced as a *[mutilated victory](/wiki/Mutilated_victory "Mutilated victory")*. The rhetoric of *mutilated victory* was adopted by Mussolini and led to the [rise of](/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)") [Italian fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism"), becoming a key point in the [propaganda of Fascist Italy](/wiki/Propaganda_of_Fascist_Italy "Propaganda of Fascist Italy"). Historians regard *mutilated victory* as a "political myth", used by fascists to fuel [Italian imperialism](/wiki/Italian_imperialism "Italian imperialism") and obscure the successes of [liberal Italy](/wiki/Liberal_Italy "Liberal Italy") in the aftermath of World War I.[[102]](#cite_note-107) Italy also gained a permanent seat in the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")'s executive council.

### Fascist regime, World War II, and Civil War (1922–1946)

Main article: [Fascist Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy "Fascist Italy")

Main article: [Italian Fascism](/wiki/Italian_Fascism "Italian Fascism")

#### Rise of Fascism into power

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg/250px-Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mussolini_mezzobusto.jpg)

[Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), who titled himself *[Duce](/wiki/Duce "Duce")* ruled the country from 1922 to1943.

[Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") created the *[Fasci di Combattimento](/wiki/Fasci_di_Combattimento "Fasci di Combattimento")* or Combat League in 1919. It was originally dominated by patriotic socialist and [syndicalist](/wiki/Syndicalist "Syndicalist") veterans who opposed the pacifist policies of the Italian Socialist Party. This early Fascist movement had a platform more inclined to the left, promising social revolution, proportional representation in elections, women's suffrage (partly realized in 1925) and dividing rural private property held by estates.[[103]](#cite_note-108)[[104]](#cite_note-109) They also differed from later Fascism by opposing [censorship](/wiki/Censorship "Censorship"), [militarism](/wiki/Militarism "Militarism") and [dictatorship](/wiki/Dictatorship "Dictatorship").[[105]](#cite_note-Smith_1997,_pp._284-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Naples_Fascist_rally_on_24_October_1922.jpg/250px-Naples_Fascist_rally_on_24_October_1922.jpg)](/wiki/File:Naples_Fascist_rally_on_24_October_1922.jpg)

[Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") during the [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome") in 1922

At the same time, the so-called *[Biennio Rosso](/wiki/Biennio_Rosso "Biennio Rosso")* ("Red Biennium" or "Two Red Years") took place in the two years following the war in a context of economic crisis, high unemployment and political instability. The 1919–20 period was characterized by mass strikes, worker manifestations as well as self-management experiments through land and factory occupations. In [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") and [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [workers councils](/wiki/Workers_councils "Workers councils") were formed and many [factory occupations](/wiki/Factory_occupations "Factory occupations") took place under the leadership of [anarcho-syndicalists](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalist "Anarcho-syndicalist"). The agitations also extended to the agricultural areas of the [Padan plain](/wiki/Padan_plain "Padan plain") and were accompanied by peasant strikes, rural unrests and guerilla conflicts between left-wing and right-wing militias. Thenceforth, the Fasci di Combattimento (forerunner of the [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party"), 1921) successfully exploited the claims of Italian nationalists and the quest for order and normalization of the middle class. In October 1922, Mussolini took advantage of a general strike to announce his demands to the Italian government to give the Fascist Party political power or face a coup. With no immediate response, a group of 30,000 Fascists began a long trek across Italy to Rome (the [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome")), claiming that Fascists were intending to restore law and order. The Fascists demanded Prime Minister [Luigi Facta](/wiki/Luigi_Facta "Luigi Facta")'s resignation and that Mussolini be named to the post. Although the Italian Army was far better armed than the Fascist militias, the liberal system and King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel III of Italy") were facing a deeper political crisis. The King was forced to choose which of the two rival movements in Italy would form the government: Mussolini's Fascists, or the marxist [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party"). He selected the Fascists.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Giacomo_Matteotti_crop.jpg/250px-Giacomo_Matteotti_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giacomo_Matteotti_crop.jpg)

Socialist leader [Giacomo Matteotti](/wiki/Giacomo_Matteotti "Giacomo Matteotti") was murdered a few days after he openly denounced Fascist violence during the 1924 elections.

Mussolini formed a coalition with nationalists and liberals, and in 1923 passed the electoral [Acerbo Law](/wiki/Acerbo_Law "Acerbo Law"), which assigned two-thirds of the seats to the party that achieved at least 25% of the vote. The Fascist Party used violence and intimidation to achieve the threshold in the [1924 election](/wiki/Italian_general_election,_1924 "Italian general election, 1924"), thus obtaining control of Parliament. Socialist deputy [Giacomo Matteotti](/wiki/Giacomo_Matteotti "Giacomo Matteotti") was assassinated after calling for a nullification of the vote. The parliament opposition responded to Matteotti's assassination with the [Aventine Secession](/wiki/Aventine_Secession_(20th_century) "Aventine Secession (20th century)").

Over the next four years, Mussolini eliminated nearly all checks and balances on his power. On 24 December 1925, he passed a law that declared he was responsible to the king alone, making him the sole person able to determine Parliament's agenda. Local governments were dissolved, and appointed officials (called *[podestà](/wiki/Podest%C3%A0 "Podestà")*) replaced elected mayors and councils. In 1928, all political parties were banned, and parliamentary elections were replaced by plebiscites in which the Grand Council of Fascism nominated a single list of 400 candidates. [Christopher Duggan](/wiki/Christopher_Duggan "Christopher Duggan") argues that his regime exploited Mussolini's popular appeal and forged a cult of personality that served as the model that was emulated by dictators of other fascist regimes of the 1930s.[[106]](#cite_note-111)

In summary, historian [Stanley G. Payne](/wiki/Stanley_G._Payne "Stanley G. Payne") says that Fascism in Italy was:

:   A primarily political dictatorship. The Fascist Party itself had become almost completely bureaucratized and subservient to, not dominant over, the state itself. Big business, industry, and finance retained extensive autonomy, particularly in the early years. The armed forces also enjoyed considerable autonomy. ... The Fascist militia was placed under military control. The judicial system was left largely intact and relatively autonomous as well. The police continued to be directed by state officials and were not taken over by party leaders, nor was a major new police elite created. There was never any question of bringing the Church under overall subservience. Sizable sectors of Italian cultural life retained extensive autonomy, and no major state propaganda-and-culture ministry existed. The Mussolini regime was neither especially sanguinary nor particularly repressive.[[107]](#cite_note-112)

#### End of the Roman question

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Group_of_Vatican_and_Italian_government_notables_posing_at_the_Lateran_Palace_before_the_signing_of_the_treaty.jpg/250px-Group_of_Vatican_and_Italian_government_notables_posing_at_the_Lateran_Palace_before_the_signing_of_the_treaty.jpg)](/wiki/File:Group_of_Vatican_and_Italian_government_notables_posing_at_the_Lateran_Palace_before_the_signing_of_the_treaty.jpg)

Vatican and Italian delegations prior to signing the [Lateran Treaty](/wiki/Lateran_Treaty "Lateran Treaty")

During the [unification of Italy](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") in the mid-19th century, the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States") resisted incorporation into the new nation. The nascent Kingdom of Italy invaded and occupied [Romagna](/wiki/Romagna "Romagna") (the eastern portion of the Papal States) in 1860, leaving only [Latium](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio") in the pope's domains. Latium, including Rome itself, was [occupied](/wiki/Capture_of_Rome "Capture of Rome") and annexed in 1870. For the following sixty years, relations between the Papacy and the Italian government were hostile, and the status of the pope became known as the "[Roman question](/wiki/Roman_question "Roman question")".
The [Lateran Treaty](/wiki/Lateran_Treaty "Lateran Treaty") was one component of the Lateran Pacts of 1929, agreements between the Kingdom of Italy under King [Victor Emmanuel III of Italy](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel III of Italy") and the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") under [Pope Pius XI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XI "Pope Pius XI") to settle the question. The treaty and associated pacts were signed on 11 February 1929.[[108]](#cite_note-SignDate-113) The treaty recognized [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City") as an [independent state](/wiki/Independent_state "Independent state") under the sovereignty of the Holy See. The Italian government also agreed to give the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") financial compensation for the loss of the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States").[[109]](#cite_note-114) In 1948, the Lateran Treaty was recognized in the [Constitution of Italy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy") as regulating the relations between the state and the Catholic Church.[[110]](#cite_note-constitution7-115) The treaty was significantly revised in 1984, ending the status of Catholicism as the sole state religion.

#### Foreign politics

Further information: [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire") and [Italian irredentism](/wiki/Italian_irredentism "Italian irredentism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/RegioniIrredenteItalia.jpg/250px-RegioniIrredenteItalia.jpg)](/wiki/File:RegioniIrredenteItalia.jpg)

Italian ethnic regions claimed by the [Italian irredentism](/wiki/Italian_irredentism "Italian irredentism") in the 1930s: \* Green: [Nice](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Nice "Italian irredentism in Nice"), [Ticino](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Switzerland "Italian irredentism in Switzerland") and [Dalmatia](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Dalmatia "Italian irredentism in Dalmatia") \* Red: [Malta](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Malta "Italian irredentism in Malta") \* Violet: [Corsica](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Corsica "Italian irredentism in Corsica") \* [Savoy](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Savoy "Italian irredentism in Savoy") and [Corfu](/wiki/Corfiot_Italians#Corfiot_Italians_and_the_Risorgimento "Corfiot Italians") were later claimed.

Lee identifies three major themes in Mussolini's foreign policy. The first was a continuation of the foreign-policy objectives of the preceding Liberal regime. Liberal Italy had allied itself with Germany and Austria, and had great ambitions in the Balkans and North Africa. Ever since it had been badly defeated in Ethiopia in 1896, there was a strong demand for seizing that country. Second was a profound disillusionment after the heavy losses of the First World War; the small territorial gains from Austria were not enough to compensate. Third was Mussolini's promise to restore the pride and glory of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").[[111]](#cite_note-116)

Italian Fascism is based upon [Italian nationalism](/wiki/Italian_nationalism "Italian nationalism") and in particular seeks to complete what it considers as the incomplete project of *[Risorgimento](/wiki/Risorgimento "Risorgimento")* by incorporating *[Italia Irredenta](/wiki/Italian_irredentism "Italian irredentism")* (unredeemed Italy) into the state of Italy.[[112]](#cite_note-autogenerated1922-117)[[113]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-118) To the east of Italy, the Fascists claimed that [Dalmatia](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Dalmatia "Italian irredentism in Dalmatia") was a land of Italian culture.[[114]](#cite_note-119) To the south of Italy, the Fascists claimed [Malta](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Malta "Italian irredentism in Malta"), which belonged to the United Kingdom, and [Corfu](/wiki/Corfiot_Italians#Corfiot_Italians_and_the_Risorgimento "Corfiot Italians"), which belonged to Greece, to the north claimed [Italian Switzerland](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Switzerland "Italian irredentism in Switzerland"), while to the west claimed [Corsica](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Corsica "Italian irredentism in Corsica"), [Nice](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Nice "Italian irredentism in Nice") and [Savoy](/wiki/Italian_irredentism_in_Savoy "Italian irredentism in Savoy"), which belonged to France.[[115]](#cite_note-120)[[116]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png/250px-Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png)](/wiki/File:Italy_aims_Europe_1936.png)

Ambitions of Fascist Italy in Europe in 1936.  
Legend:

Metropolitan Italy and dependent territories;

[Client states](/wiki/Client_state "Client state");

Claimed territories to be annexed;

Territories to be transformed into client states.

[Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), which was a client state, was considered a territory to be annexed.

Mussolini promised to bring Italy back as a [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") in Europe, building a "New Roman Empire" and holding power over the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"). In [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda_in_Fascist_Italy "Propaganda in Fascist Italy"), Fascists used the ancient Roman motto "*[Mare Nostrum](/wiki/Mare_Nostrum "Mare Nostrum")*" ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") for "Our Sea") to describe the Mediterranean. For this reason the Fascist regime engaged in [interventionist foreign policy](/wiki/Interventionism_(politics) "Interventionism (politics)") in Europe. In 1923, the Greek island of [Corfu](/wiki/Corfiot_Italians#Corfiot_Italians_and_the_Risorgimento "Corfiot Italians") was briefly occupied by Italy, after the assassination of [General Tellini](/wiki/Enrico_Tellini "Enrico Tellini") in Greek territory. In 1925, [Albania](/wiki/Albanian_Republic_(1925%E2%80%931928) "Albanian Republic (1925–1928)") came under heavy Italian influence as a result of the [Tirana Treaties](/wiki/First_and_Second_Treaties_of_Tirana "First and Second Treaties of Tirana"), which also gave Italy a stronger position in the Balkans.[[117]](#cite_note-122) Relations with France were mixed. The Fascist regime planned to regain Italian-populated areas of France.[[118]](#cite_note-Smith_3-123) With the rise of Nazism, it became more concerned of the potential threat of Germany to Italy. Due to concerns of German expansionism, Italy joined the [Stresa Front](/wiki/Stresa_Front "Stresa Front") with France and the United Kingdom, which existed from 1935 to 1936. The Fascist regime held negative relations with Yugoslavia, as it continued to claim Dalmatia.

During the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War") between the socialist [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Republican faction (Spanish Civil War)") and [Nationalists](/wiki/Nationalist_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Nationalist faction (Spanish Civil War)") led by [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco"), Italy sent arms and over 60,000 troops to aid the Nationalist faction. This secured Italy's naval access to Spanish ports and increased Italian influence in the Mediterranean. During the 1930s, Italy strongly pursued a policy of naval rearmament; by 1940, the *[Regia Marina](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina")* was the fourth largest navy in the world.

[![Chamberlain, Daladier, Hitler, Mussolini, and Italian foreign minister Count Ciano, as they prepared to sign the Munich Agreement](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R69173%2C_M%C3%BCnchener_Abkommen%2C_Staatschefs.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R69173%2C_M%C3%BCnchener_Abkommen%2C_Staatschefs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R69173,_M%C3%BCnchener_Abkommen,_Staatschefs.jpg)

From left to right: Chamberlain, Daladier, Hitler, Mussolini and Italian foreign minister [Count Ciano](/wiki/Count_Ciano "Count Ciano") at the signing of [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement")

Mussolini and [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") first met in June 1934, when Mussolini opposed German plans to annex Austria to ensure that Nazi Germany would not become hegemonic in Europe. Public appearances and propaganda constantly portrayed the closeness of Mussolini and Hitler and the similarities between Italian Fascism and German [National Socialism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism"). While both ideologies had significant similarities, the two factions were suspicious of each other, and both leaders were in competition for world influence.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Hitler_and_Mussolini_June_1940.jpg/250px-Hitler_and_Mussolini_June_1940.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitler_and_Mussolini_June_1940.jpg)

Mussolini and Hitler in June 1940

After completing the pacification of Libya, Mussolini decided to launch the war in [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire"), one of the few uncolonized African territories. The [Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Second Italo-Ethiopian War"), which began in October 1935, was characterized by the massive use of force and the use of chemical weapons against the Ethiopians. Victory, announced in 9 May 1936 after the entry of Italian troops into [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"), led to the proclamation of the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"). However, the war resulted in the international isolation of Italy; the only nation to back Italy's aggression was Nazi Germany. After being condemned by the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations"), Italy decided to leave the League on 11 December 1937.[[119]](#cite_note-124) Mussolini had little choice but to join Hitler in international politics, thus he reluctantly abandoned support of Austrian independence and Hitler proceeded with the *[Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss")*, the annexation of Austria, in 1938. Mussolini later supported German claims on [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") at the [Munich Conference](/wiki/Munich_Conference "Munich Conference"). In 1938, under influence of Hitler, Mussolini supported the adoption of antisemitic [racial laws](/wiki/Manifesto_of_Race "Manifesto of Race") in Italy. After Germany annexed [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") in March 1939, [Italy invaded Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania") and made it an [Italian protectorate](/wiki/Italian_protectorate_of_Albania_(1939%E2%80%931943) "Italian protectorate of Albania (1939–1943)").

As war approached in 1939, the Fascist regime stepped up an aggressive press campaign against France claiming that its Italian residents were suffering.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997397-125) This was important to the alliance as both regimes mutually had claims on France: Germany on German-populated [Alsace-Lorraine](/wiki/Alsace-Lorraine "Alsace-Lorraine") and Italy on the mixed Italian and French populated [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") and [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica"). In May 1939, a formal alliance with Germany was signed, known as the [Pact of Steel](/wiki/Pact_of_Steel "Pact of Steel"). Mussolini felt obliged to sign the pact in spite of his own concerns that Italy could not fight a war in the near future. This obligation grew from his promises to Italians that he would build an empire for them and from his personal desire to not allow Hitler to become the dominant leader in Europe.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997401-126) Mussolini was repulsed by the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact") agreement where Germany and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") agreed to partition the [Second Polish Republic](/wiki/Second_Polish_Republic "Second Polish Republic") into German and Soviet zones for an impending invasion. The Fascist government saw this as a betrayal of the [Anti-Comintern Pact](/wiki/Anti-Comintern_Pact "Anti-Comintern Pact"), but decided to remain officially silent.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997401-126)

#### World War II and fall of Fascism

Main articles: [Military history of Italy during World War II](/wiki/Military_history_of_Italy_during_World_War_II "Military history of Italy during World War II") and [Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/ProgettoImperoItaliano.jpg/250px-ProgettoImperoItaliano.jpg)](/wiki/File:ProgettoImperoItaliano.jpg)

Map of *Great Italy* according to the 1940 fascist project in case Italy had won the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War") (The orange line delimits metropolitan Italy, the green line the borders of the enlarged [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire").)

When Germany [invaded Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") on 1 September 1939 beginning [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Mussolini chose to stay [non-belligerent](/wiki/Non-belligerent "Non-belligerent"), although he declared his support for Hitler. In drawing out war plans, Mussolini and the Fascist regime decided that Italy would aim to annex large portions of Africa and the Middle East. Hesitance remained from the King and military commander [Pietro Badoglio](/wiki/Pietro_Badoglio "Pietro Badoglio") who warned Mussolini that Italy had too few [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank"), [armoured vehicles](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)"), and aircraft available to be able to carry out a long-term war; Badoglio told Mussolini "It is suicide" for Italy to get involved in the [European conflict](/wiki/European_Theater_of_World_War_II "European Theater of World War II").[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997405-127) Mussolini and the Fascist regime thus waited as France was invaded by Germany in June 1940 ([Battle of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France")) before deciding to get involved.

Italy entered the war on 10 June 1940, fulfilling its obligations towards the Pact of Steel. Mussolini hoped to quickly capture [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy"), Nice, Corsica, and the African colonies of Tunisia and Algeria from the French, on 21 June, Italy [invaded France](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_France "Italian invasion of France") but their achieved with only modest gains due to the [Franco-Italian Armistice](/wiki/Franco-Italian_Armistice "Franco-Italian Armistice") on 24 June, but Germany signed an armistice (22 June: [Second Armistice at Compiègne](/wiki/Second_Armistice_at_Compi%C3%A8gne "Second Armistice at Compiègne")) with Marshal [Philippe Pétain](/wiki/Philippe_P%C3%A9tain "Philippe Pétain") establishing [Vichy France](/wiki/Vichy_France "Vichy France"), that retained control over southern France and colonies. This decision angered the Fascist regime.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997406-128) In summer 1940, Mussolini ordered the [bombing of Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Italian_bombing_of_Mandatory_Palestine_in_World_War_II "Italian bombing of Mandatory Palestine in World War II") and the [conquest of British Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_conquest_of_British_Somaliland "Italian conquest of British Somaliland"). In September, he ordered the [invasion of Egypt](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Egypt "Italian invasion of Egypt"); despite initial success, Italian forces were soon driven back by the British on [Operation Compass](/wiki/Operation_Compass "Operation Compass"). Hitler had to intervene with the sending of the [Afrika Korps](/wiki/Afrika_Korps "Afrika Korps") of General [Erwin Rommel](/wiki/Erwin_Rommel "Erwin Rommel"), that was the mainstay in the [North African campaign](/wiki/North_African_campaign "North African campaign"). Although Rommel himself was officially under Italian command.

On 28 October, Mussolini launched [an attack](/wiki/Greco-Italian_War "Greco-Italian War") on Greece. However, the Greeks not only proved a more capable opponent successfully repelling the initial attack, but also managed to push the Italians back to Albania. Hitler came to Mussolini's aid by attacking the Greeks through the Balkans. The [Balkans Campaign](/wiki/Balkans_Campaign_(World_War_II) "Balkans Campaign (World War II)") had as result the dissolution of Yugoslavia and Greece's defeat. Italy gained [south Slovenia](/wiki/Province_of_Ljubljana "Province of Ljubljana"), [Dalmatia](/wiki/Governorate_of_Dalmatia "Governorate of Dalmatia"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Italian_governorate_of_Montenegro "Italian governorate of Montenegro") and established the puppet states of [Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia") and [Hellenic State](/wiki/Hellenic_State "Hellenic State"). By 1942, it was faltering as its economy failed to adapt to the conditions of war and Italian cities were being heavily bombed by the Allies. Also, despite the Axis advances, the campaign in North Africa began to fail in late 1942. The complete collapse came after the decisive defeat at [El Alamein](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_El_Alamein "Second Battle of El Alamein").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Italian_troops_charging_in_North_Africa_%28retouched%29.jpg/250px-Italian_troops_charging_in_North_Africa_%28retouched%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_troops_charging_in_North_Africa_(retouched).jpg)

Italian troops in North Africa

By 1943, Italy was losing on every front. Half of the Italian forces [fighting in the Soviet Union](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(World_War_II) "Eastern Front (World War II)") had been destroyed,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997412-129) the African campaign had failed, the Balkans remained unstable, and Italians wanted an end to the war.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1997412–413-130) In July 1943, the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") [invaded Sicily](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Sicily "Allied invasion of Sicily") in an effort to knock Italy out of the war and establish a foothold in Europe. On 25 July, [Mussolini was ousted](/wiki/25_Luglio "25 Luglio") by the [Great Council of Fascism](/wiki/Gran_Consiglio_del_Fascismo "Gran Consiglio del Fascismo") and arrested by order of King Victor Emmanuel III, who appointed General [Pietro Badoglio](/wiki/Pietro_Badoglio "Pietro Badoglio") as new [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy"). Badoglio stripped away the final elements of Fascist rule by banning the [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party"), then signed the [Armistice of Cassibile](/wiki/Armistice_of_Cassibile "Armistice of Cassibile") and the [instrument of surrender](/wiki/Italian_Instrument_of_Surrender "Italian Instrument of Surrender") on 29 September.

Donald Detwiler notes that "Italy's entrance into the war showed very early that her military strength was only a hollow shell. Italy's military failures against France, Greece, Yugoslavia and in the African Theatres of war shook Italy's new prestige mightily."[[126]](#cite_note-131) Historians have long debated why Italy's military and its Fascist regime were so remarkably ineffective at an activity – war – that was central to their identity. MacGregor Knox says the explanation, "was first and foremost a failure of Italy's military culture and military institutions."[[127]](#cite_note-132) Norman Polmar and Thomas B. Allen argue that "the Regia Aeronautica failed to perform effectively in modern conflict."[[128]](#cite_note-133) James Sadkovich gives the most charitable interpretation of Italian failures, blaming inferior equipment, overextension, and inter-service rivalries. Its forces had "more than their share of handicaps."[[129]](#cite_note-134)

#### Civil War, Allied advance, and Liberation

Further information: [Italian Civil War](/wiki/Italian_Civil_War "Italian Civil War") and [Liberation of Italy](/wiki/Liberation_of_Italy "Liberation of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Flag_of_Italian_Committee_of_National_Liberation.svg/250px-Flag_of_Italian_Committee_of_National_Liberation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Italian_Committee_of_National_Liberation.svg)

Flag of the [National Liberation Committee](/wiki/National_Liberation_Committee "National Liberation Committee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Quattrogiornate.jpg/250px-Quattrogiornate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quattrogiornate.jpg)

Insurgents celebrating the liberation of Naples, after the [Four days of Naples](/wiki/Four_days_of_Naples "Four days of Naples") (27–30 September 1943)

Soon after being ousted, Mussolini was rescued by a German commando in [Operation Eiche](/wiki/Gran_Sasso_raid "Gran Sasso raid") ("Oak"). The Germans brought Mussolini to northern Italy where he set up a Fascist puppet state, the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") (RSI). Meanwhile, the Allies advanced in southern Italy. In September 1943, [Naples](/wiki/Four_days_of_Naples "Four days of Naples") rose against the occupying German forces. The Allies organized some royalist Italian troops into the [Italian Co-Belligerent Army](/wiki/Italian_Co-Belligerent_Army "Italian Co-Belligerent Army"), while other troops continued to fight alongside Nazi Germany in the *Esercito Nazionale Repubblicano*, the [National Republican Army](/wiki/National_Republican_Army "National Republican Army"). A large [Italian resistance movement](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement") started a long [guerrilla war](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") against the German and Fascist forces,[[130]](#cite_note-135) while clashes between the Fascist RSI Army and the Royalist Italian Co-Belligerent Army were rare.[[131]](#cite_note-136) The Germans, often helped by Fascists, committed several [atrocities](/wiki/War_crimes_of_the_Wehrmacht "War crimes of the Wehrmacht") against Italian civilians in occupied zones, such as the [Ardeatine massacre](/wiki/Ardeatine_massacre "Ardeatine massacre") and the [Sant'Anna di Stazzema massacre](/wiki/Sant%27Anna_di_Stazzema_massacre "Sant'Anna di Stazzema massacre"). The Kingdom of Italy declared war on Nazi Germany on 13 October 1943;[[132]](#cite_note-italyswitchesstayinwar-137)[[133]](#cite_note-138) tensions between the Axis Powers and the Italian military were rising following the failure to defend Sicily.[[132]](#cite_note-italyswitchesstayinwar-137)

On 4 June 1944, the German occupation of Rome came to an end as the Allies advanced. The final Allied victory over the Axis in Italy did not come until the spring offensive of 1945, after Allied troops had breached the [Gothic Line](/wiki/Gothic_Line "Gothic Line"), leading to the surrender of German and Fascist forces in Italy on 2 May shortly before Germany finally surrendered ending World War II in Europe on 8 May. It is estimated that between September 1943 and April 1945, some 60,000 Allied and 50,000 German soldiers died in Italy.[[c]](#cite_note-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-316-1181-11%2C_Italien%2C_Benito_Mussolini_mit_italienischen_Soldaten.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-316-1181-11%2C_Italien%2C_Benito_Mussolini_mit_italienischen_Soldaten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_101I-316-1181-11,_Italien,_Benito_Mussolini_mit_italienischen_Soldaten.jpg)

The head of the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic"), [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), with a soldier in 1944

During World War II, [Italian war crimes](/wiki/Italian_war_crimes "Italian war crimes") included [extrajudicial killings](/wiki/Extrajudicial_killing "Extrajudicial killing") and [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing")[[135]](#cite_note-JamesBurgwyn_2004-141) by the deportation of about 25,000 people, mainly Jews, Croats, and Slovenians, to the [Italian concentration camps](/wiki/List_of_Italian_concentration_camps "List of Italian concentration camps"), such as [Rab](/wiki/Rab_concentration_camp "Rab concentration camp"), [Gonars](/wiki/Gonars_concentration_camp "Gonars concentration camp"), [Monigo](/wiki/Monigo "Monigo"), [Renicci di Anghiari](/wiki/Renicci_di_Anghiari "Renicci di Anghiari") and elsewhere. [Yugoslav Partisans](/wiki/Yugoslav_Partisans "Yugoslav Partisans") perpetrated their own crimes against the local ethnic Italian population during and after the war, including the [foibe massacres](/wiki/Foibe_massacres "Foibe massacres"). In Italy and Yugoslavia, unlike in Germany, few war crimes were prosecuted.[[136]](#cite_note-RoryCarroll_2001-142)[[137]](#cite_note-Pedaliu_JContHistory-143)[[138]](#cite_note-oliva06-144)[[139]](#cite_note-blpp2004-145)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/01_partigiani_a_milano1.jpg/250px-01_partigiani_a_milano1.jpg)](/wiki/File:01_partigiani_a_milano1.jpg)

[Italian anti-fascist partisans](/wiki/Italian_resistance_movement "Italian resistance movement") in Milan during the [liberation of Italy](/wiki/Liberation_of_Italy "Liberation of Italy"), April 1945

On 25 April 1945 the [National Liberation Committee for Northern Italy](/wiki/National_Liberation_Committee_for_Northern_Italy "National Liberation Committee for Northern Italy") proclaimed a general insurrection in all the territories still occupied by the Nazis, indicating to all the partisan forces active in Northern Italy that were part of the Volunteer Corps of Freedom to attack the fascist and German garrisons by imposing the surrender, days before the arrival of the Allied troops; at the same time, the National Liberation Committee for Northern Italy personally issued legislative decrees,[[140]](#cite_note-146) assuming power "in the name of the Italian people and as a delegate of the Italian Government", establishing among other things the death sentence for all fascist hierarchs,[[141]](#cite_note-147) Today the event is commemorated in Italy every 25 April by the [Liberation Day](/wiki/Liberation_Day_(Italy) "Liberation Day (Italy)"), [National Day](/wiki/National_Day "National Day") introduced on 22 April 1946, which celebrates the liberation of the country from [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism").[[142]](#cite_note-148)

Mussolini was captured on 27 April 1945, by [communist](/wiki/Partito_Comunista_Italiano "Partito Comunista Italiano") [Italian partisans](/wiki/Italian_partisans "Italian partisans") near the Swiss border as he tried to escape Italy. On the next day, he was executed for high treason. Days later on 2 May 1945, the German forces in Italy surrendered. On 9 June 1944, Badoglio was replaced as prime minister by anti-fascist leader [Ivanoe Bonomi](/wiki/Ivanoe_Bonomi "Ivanoe Bonomi"). In June 1945 Bonomi was in turn replaced by [Ferruccio Parri](/wiki/Ferruccio_Parri "Ferruccio Parri"), who in turn gave way to [Alcide de Gasperi](/wiki/Alcide_de_Gasperi "Alcide de Gasperi") on 4 December 1945. Finally, De Gasperi supervised the transition to a Republic following the abdication of Vittorio Emanuele III on 9 May 1946, the one-month-long reign of his son [Umberto II](/wiki/Umberto_II_of_Italy "Umberto II of Italy") ("King of May") and the [Constitutional Referendum](/wiki/Italian_constitutional_referendum,_1946 "Italian constitutional referendum, 1946") that abolished the monarchy; De Gasperi briefly became acting head of state as well as prime minister on 18 June 1946, but ceded the former role to Provisional President [Enrico de Nicola](/wiki/Enrico_de_Nicola "Enrico de Nicola") ten days later.

### Anti-fascism against Mussolini's regime

See also: [Anti-fascism](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Flag_of_the_Arditi_del_Popolo_Battalion.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Arditi_del_Popolo_Battalion.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Arditi_del_Popolo_Battalion.svg)

Flag of *[Arditi del Popolo](/wiki/Arditi_del_Popolo "Arditi del Popolo")*, an axe cutting a [fasces](/wiki/Fasces "Fasces"). *Arditi del Popolo* was a militant [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism") group founded in 1921.

In Italy, Mussolini's [Fascist](/wiki/Italian_Fascism "Italian Fascism") regime used the term *[anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism")* to describe its opponents. Mussolini's [secret police](/wiki/Secret_police "Secret police") was officially known as the [Organization for Vigilance and Repression of Anti-Fascism](/wiki/Organization_for_Vigilance_and_Repression_of_Anti-Fascism "Organization for Vigilance and Repression of Anti-Fascism") (OVRA). During the 1920s, anti-fascists, many of them from the [labor movement](/wiki/Labor_movement "Labor movement"), fought against the violent [Blackshirts](/wiki/Blackshirts "Blackshirts") and against the rise of the fascist leader Benito Mussolini. After the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") (PSI) signed a [pacification pact](/wiki/Pact_of_Pacification "Pact of Pacification") with Mussolini and his [Fasces of Combat](/wiki/Fasci_Italiani_di_Combattimento "Fasci Italiani di Combattimento") on 3 August 1921,[[143]](#cite_note-149) and trade unions adopted a legalist and pacified strategy, members of the workers' movement who disagreed with this strategy formed *[Arditi del Popolo](/wiki/Arditi_del_Popolo "Arditi del Popolo")*.[[144]](#cite_note-150)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Concentrazione_di_azione_antifascista.jpg/250px-Concentrazione_di_azione_antifascista.jpg)](/wiki/File:Concentrazione_di_azione_antifascista.jpg)

1931 badge of a member of [Concentrazione Antifascista Italiana](/wiki/Concentrazione_Antifascista_Italiana "Concentrazione Antifascista Italiana")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Flag_of_Giustizia_e_Liberta.svg/250px-Flag_of_Giustizia_e_Liberta.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_Giustizia_e_Liberta.svg)

Flag of *[Giustizia e Libertà](/wiki/Giustizia_e_Libert%C3%A0 "Giustizia e Libertà")*, anti-fascist movement active from 1929 to 1945

The [Italian General Confederation of Labour](/wiki/Italian_General_Confederation_of_Labour "Italian General Confederation of Labour") (CGL) and the PSI refused to officially recognize the anti-fascist militia and maintained a non-violent, legalist strategy, while the [Communist Party of Italy](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Italy "Communist Party of Italy") (PCd'I) ordered its members to quit the organization. The PCd'I organized some militant groups, but their actions were relatively minor.[[145]](#cite_note-151) The Italian anarchist [Severino Di Giovanni](/wiki/Severino_Di_Giovanni "Severino Di Giovanni"), who exiled himself to Argentina following the 1922 [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome"), organized several bombings against the Italian fascist community.[[146]](#cite_note-152) The Italian liberal anti-fascist [Benedetto Croce](/wiki/Benedetto_Croce "Benedetto Croce") wrote his *[Manifesto of the Anti-Fascist Intellectuals](/wiki/Manifesto_of_the_Anti-Fascist_Intellectuals "Manifesto of the Anti-Fascist Intellectuals")*, which was published in 1925.[[147]](#cite_note-153) Other notable Italian liberal anti-fascists around that time were [Piero Gobetti](/wiki/Piero_Gobetti "Piero Gobetti") and [Carlo Rosselli](/wiki/Carlo_Rosselli "Carlo Rosselli").[[148]](#cite_note-154)

*[Concentrazione Antifascista Italiana](/wiki/Concentrazione_Antifascista_Italiana "Concentrazione Antifascista Italiana")* (English: Italian Anti-Fascist Concentration), officially known as Concentrazione d'Azione Antifascista (Anti-Fascist Action Concentration), was an Italian coalition of Anti-Fascist groups which existed from 1927 to 1934, trying to promote and to coordinate expatriate actions to fight fascism in Italy; they published a propaganda paper entitled *La Libertà*.[[149]](#cite_note-155)[[150]](#cite_note-156)[[151]](#cite_note-157) *[Giustizia e Libertà](/wiki/Giustizia_e_Libert%C3%A0 "Giustizia e Libertà")* (English: Justice and Freedom) was an Italian [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism") [resistance movement](/wiki/Resistance_movement "Resistance movement"), active from 1929 to 1945[[152]](#cite_note-jam-158) which shared a belief in active, effective opposition to fascism, compared to the older Italian anti-fascist parties. *Giustizia e Libertà* also made the international community aware of the realities of fascism in Italy, thanks to the work of [Gaetano Salvemini](/wiki/Gaetano_Salvemini "Gaetano Salvemini").

Between 1920 and 1943, several anti-fascist movements were active among the [Slovenes](/wiki/Slovenes "Slovenes") and [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats") in the territories annexed to Italy after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), known as the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March").[[153]](#cite_note-159)[[154]](#cite_note-160) The most influential was the militant insurgent organization [TIGR](/wiki/TIGR "TIGR"), which carried out numerous sabotages, as well as attacks on representatives of the Fascist Party and the military.[[155]](#cite_note-161)[[156]](#cite_note-162) Most of the underground structure of the organization was discovered and dismantled by the OVRA in 1940 and 1941,[[157]](#cite_note-163) and after June 1941 most of its former activists joined the [Slovene Partisans](/wiki/Slovene_Partisans "Slovene Partisans"). Many members of the [Italian resistance](/wiki/Italian_resistance "Italian resistance") left their homes and went to live in the mountains, fighting against Italian fascists and [German Nazi](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") soldiers during the [Italian Civil War](/wiki/Italian_Civil_War "Italian Civil War"). Many cities in Italy, including [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") and [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), were freed by anti-fascist uprisings.[[158]](#cite_note-164)

### End of the Kingdom of Italy (1946)

#### 1946 Italian institutional referendum

Main article: [1946 Italian institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Hrh_Prince_Umberto_of_Italy%2C_May_1944_TR1829.jpg/250px-Hrh_Prince_Umberto_of_Italy%2C_May_1944_TR1829.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hrh_Prince_Umberto_of_Italy,_May_1944_TR1829.jpg)

[Umberto II](/wiki/Umberto_II "Umberto II"), the last [king of Italy](/wiki/King_of_Italy "King of Italy")

Much like [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), the aftermath of World War II left Italy with a destroyed economy, a divided society, and anger against the monarchy for its endorsement of the Fascist regime for the previous twenty years.

Even before the rise of the Fascists, the monarchy was seen to have performed poorly, with society extremely divided between the wealthy North and poor South. World War I resulted in Italy making few gains and was seen as what fostered the rise of Fascism. These frustrations contributed to a revival of the Italian republican movement.[[159]](#cite_note-165) By the spring of 1944, it was obvious Victor Emmanuel was too tainted by his previous support for Mussolini to have any further role. He transferred his constitutional powers to Crown Prince Umberto, whom he named [Lieutenant General of the Realm](/wiki/Luogotenente "Luogotenente") and de facto regent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Italian_referendum_1946_support_for_republic.svg/250px-Italian_referendum_1946_support_for_republic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italian_referendum_1946_support_for_republic.svg)

Results of the [1946 referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_constitutional_referendum "1946 Italian constitutional referendum")

Victor Emmanuel III nominally remained King until shortly before the [1946 Italian institutional referendum](/wiki/1946_Italian_institutional_referendum "1946 Italian institutional referendum") on whether to remain a monarchy or become a republic. On 9 May 1946, he abdicated in favour of the Crown Prince, who then ascended as King [Umberto II](/wiki/Umberto_II_of_Italy "Umberto II of Italy").
