However, on 2 June 1946, the republican side won 54% of the vote, and Italy officially became a republic, a day celebrated since as *[Festa della Repubblica](/wiki/Festa_della_Repubblica "Festa della Repubblica")*. This was the first time that Italian women voted at the national level, and the second time overall considering the local elections that were held a few months earlier in some cities.[[160]](#cite_note-166)[[161]](#cite_note-167)

The table of results shows some relevant differences in the different parts of Italy. The peninsula seemed to be drastically cut into two as if there were two different homogeneous countries: the North for the republic (with 66.2%); the South for the monarchy (with 63.8%). Some monarchist groups claimed that there was manipulation by northern republicans, socialists and communists. Others argued that Italy was still too chaotic in 1946 to have an accurate referendum.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Umberto II decided to leave Italy on 13 June to avoid the clashes between monarchists and republicans, already manifested in bloody events in various Italian cities, for fear they could extend throughout the country. He went into exile in [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal").[[162]](#cite_note-168) From 1 January 1948, with the entry into force of the [Constitution of the Italian Republic](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy"), the male descendants of Umberto II of Savoy were banned from entering Italy; the provision being repealed in 2002.[[163]](#cite_note-169)

#### Aftermath

Main articles: [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic "Italian Republic") and [History of the Italian Republic](/wiki/History_of_the_Italian_Republic "History of the Italian Republic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Alcide_de_Gasperi_2.jpg/250px-Alcide_de_Gasperi_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alcide_de_Gasperi_2.jpg)

[Alcide De Gasperi](/wiki/Alcide_De_Gasperi "Alcide De Gasperi"), [first](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_Italy "List of Prime Ministers of Italy") republican [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") and one of the [founding fathers of the European Union](/wiki/Founding_fathers_of_the_European_Union "Founding fathers of the European Union")

The [Republican Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy"), resulting from the work of a [Constituent Assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly_of_Italy "Constituent Assembly of Italy") formed by the representatives of all the [anti-fascist](/wiki/Anti-fascist "Anti-fascist") forces that contributed to the defeat of Nazi and Fascist forces during the [liberation of Italy](/wiki/Liberation_of_Italy "Liberation of Italy"),[[164]](#cite_note-170) was approved on 1 January 1948.

Under the [Treaty of Peace with Italy, 1947](/wiki/Treaty_of_Peace_with_Italy,_1947 "Treaty of Peace with Italy, 1947"), [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria"), [Kvarner](/wiki/Kvarner_Gulf "Kvarner Gulf"), most of the [Julian March](/wiki/Julian_March "Julian March") as well as the [Dalmatian](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") city of [Zara](/wiki/Zadar "Zadar") was annexed by [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") causing the [Istrian-Dalmatian exodus](/wiki/Istrian-Dalmatian_exodus "Istrian-Dalmatian exodus"), which led to the emigration from 1943 to 1960 of between 230,000 and 350,000 local ethnic [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians") ([Istrian Italians](/wiki/Istrian_Italians "Istrian Italians") and [Dalmatian Italians](/wiki/Dalmatian_Italians "Dalmatian Italians")), the others being ethnic Slovenians, ethnic Croatians, and ethnic [Istro-Romanians](/wiki/Istro-Romanians "Istro-Romanians"), choosing to maintain Italian citizenship.[[165]](#cite_note-171) Later, the [Free Territory of Trieste](/wiki/Free_Territory_of_Trieste "Free Territory of Trieste") was divided between the two states. Italy also lost its colonial possessions, formally ending the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire"). The Italian border that applies today has existed since 1975, when [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") was formally re-annexed to Italy.

Fears of a possible Communist takeover proved crucial for the first universal suffrage electoral outcome on [18 April 1948](/wiki/Italian_general_election,_1948 "Italian general election, 1948"), when the [Christian Democrats](/wiki/Christian_Democracy_(Italy) "Christian Democracy (Italy)"), under the leadership of [Alcide De Gasperi](/wiki/Alcide_De_Gasperi "Alcide De Gasperi"), obtained a landslide victory.[[166]](#cite_note-172)[[167]](#cite_note-173) Consequently, in 1949 Italy became a member of [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"). The [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") helped to revive the Italian economy which, until the late 1960s, enjoyed a period of sustained economic growth commonly called the "[Economic Miracle](/wiki/Italian_economic_miracle "Italian economic miracle")". In the 1950s, Italy became one of the six founding countries of the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities"), following the 1952 establishment of the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community"), and subsequent 1958 creations of the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community") and [European Atomic Energy Community](/wiki/European_Atomic_Energy_Community "European Atomic Energy Community"). In 1993, the former two of these were incorporated into the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-66)** In 1848, [Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour](/wiki/Camillo_Benso,_Count_of_Cavour "Camillo Benso, Count of Cavour") had formed a parliamentary group in the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") Parliament named the *Partito Liberale Italiano* (Italian Liberal Party). From 1860, with the [Unification of Italy](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") substantially realized and the death of Cavour himself in 1861, the Liberal Party was split into at least two major factions or new parties later known as the *Destra Storica* on the right wing, who substantially assembled the Count of Cavour's followers and political heirs; and the *Sinistra Storica* on the left wing, who mostly reunited the followers and sympathizers of [Giuseppe Garibaldi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Garibaldi "Giuseppe Garibaldi") and other former [Mazzinians](/wiki/Giuseppe_Mazzini "Giuseppe Mazzini"). The Historical Right (*Destra Storica*) and the Historical Left (*Sinistra Storica*) were composed of royalist liberals. At the same time, radicals organized themselves into the [Radical Party](/wiki/Italian_Radical_Party "Italian Radical Party") and republicans into the [Italian Republican Party](/wiki/Italian_Republican_Party "Italian Republican Party").
2. **[^](#cite_ref-67)** The liberal-conservative Historical Right was dominated from 1860 to 1876 (also after it was no more at the govern) by the leadership of elected Representatives from [Emilia-Romagna](/wiki/Emilia-Romagna "Emilia-Romagna") (1860–1864) and [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany") (1864–1876), known as the *Consorteria*, with the support of the Lombard and Southern Italian representatives. The majority of the Piemontese liberal-conservative representatives, but not all of them, organized themselves as the all-Piemontese and more right-wing party's minority: the *Associazione Liberale Permanente* (Permanent Liberal Association), who sometimes voted with the Historical Left and whose leading representative was [Quintino Sella](/wiki/Quintino_Sella "Quintino Sella"). The party's majority was also weakened by the substantial differences between the effective liberal-conservative (Toscano and Emiliano) leadership and Lombards on one side and the quietly conservative Southern and "Transigent Roman Catholic" components on the other side. ([Indro Montanelli](/wiki/Indro_Montanelli "Indro Montanelli"), *Storia d'Italia*, volume 32).
3. **[^](#cite_ref-140)** In *Alexander's Generals* Blaxland quotes 59,151 Allied deaths between 3 September 1943 and 2 May 1945 as recorded at AFHQ and gives the breakdown between 20 nationalities: United States 20,442; United Kingdom, 18,737; France, Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Senegal and Belgium 5,241; Canada, 4,798; India, Pakistan, Nepal 4,078; Poland 2,028; New Zealand 1,688; Italy (excluding irregulars) 917; South Africa 800; Brazil 275; Greece 115; [Jewish volunteers](/wiki/Jewish_Brigade "Jewish Brigade") from the [British Mandate in Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") 32. In addition, 35 soldiers were killed by enemy action while serving with pioneer units from Botswana, Lesotho, Swaziland, Seychelles, Mauritius, Sri Lanka, Lebanon, Cyprus and the West Indies[[134]](#cite_note-Blax11-139)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-174)** Provisional confederation between the pro-Savoy governments of the ex-[Grand Duchy of Tuscany](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Tuscany "Grand Duchy of Tuscany"), Emilian duchies and Pontifical Romagna, specially created to favor their union with the Kingdom of Sardinia.
